accents.title: accents
accents.content: The Accent Mark (Ο Τόνος) In Modern Greek, the accent mark (ο τόνος) is not optional. It is a crucial compass that tells you exactly how to pronounce a word. In English, you have to guess where the stress goes (e.g. " re cord" vs "re cord "). In Greek, the language hands you the answer on a silver platter. 1. The Fundamental Rules There are only three rules you need to know about accents: The Target : The accent mark only goes on top of vowels (α, ε, η, ι, υ, ο, ω). Never on a consonant! The Limit : It can only be placed on one of the last three syllables of a word. You will never see an accent mark at the very beginning of a long word. The Monosyllable Rule : Words with only one syllable (e.g., ναι = yes, όχι = no - wait, όχι has two syllables!) Words like και (and), με (with), το (the) generally do not take an accent mark. [!TIP] Exceptions to the Monosyllable Rule There are exactly two one-syllable words that do take an accent mark, just to distinguish them from identical words: - πού means "where?", while που means "that/which". - πώς means "how?", while πως means "that". 2. Double Vowels & The Accent What happens when a word has a double vowel (like ει or ου )? Which letter gets the accent mark? If the combination makes a single sound (like the [ee] in εί ναι), the accent mark always goes on the second letter. εί ναι (he is) αυτ οί (they) ού τε (neither) If you see an accent mark on the first letter of a combo, it means the combo is broken! You must pronounce both vowels separately. - μ άι ος (Ma-yos = May) - π λάι (pla-i = beside) 3. Why Accents Matter The placement of the accent isn't just about sounding correct; it can change the entire meaning of a word! English has this too (e.g., "I will de sert the de ssert"). Look at these "False Twins": νóμος (nó-mos) = law νομóς (no-mós) = prefecture/district γέρος (yé-ros) = old man γερός (ye-rós) = strong πότε (pó-te) = when? ποτέ (po-té) = never If you say "never" when you meant to say "when?", you're going to have a very confusing conversation! 🎯 Quick Review Hit the accented syllable with your voice! If a word has 2+ syllables, it must have an accent mark. If a word has 1 syllable, it almost never has an accent mark (with the exceptions of πού and πώς ). For double vowels ( ει, οι, ου ), the accent goes on the second letter.

accusative-case.title: Unit 5: The Accusative Case
accusative-case.content: The Accusative Case (Direct Objects & Movement) In Unit 2, you learned the theory behind the Accusative Case . You learned that when a noun is receiving the action (like being seen or being held), it becomes the Object. Because it is the Object, a masculine noun drops its final " -ς ", and the article changes to " τον / την / το ". Now that you know how to conjugate verbs, it's time to put the Accusative Case into practice! 1. Verbs that Demand the Accusative In Greek, almost every verb that performs an action on something requires that "something" to be in the Accusative case. Look at the verb βλέπω (I see). You can't just "see". You have to see something . Βλέπω τον δάσκαλο. (I see the teacher). Βλέπω την τηλεόραση. (I see the TV). Βλέπω το παιδί. (I see the child). [!TIP] What about "Είμαι" (I am)? The verb "To be" is a mirror. It just reflects the Subject back onto itself ("I am George"). Because nothing is being acted upon, verbs like είμαι DO NOT take the Accusative case. They keep everything in the Nominative case! - Είμαι ο Γιώργος. (I am George). 2. Prepositions of Movement (σε, από, με) The Accusative case isn't just for Direct Objects. It is also triggered by little words called Prepositions . In English, prepositions are words like in, at, to, from, with . When you use these words in Greek, the word that comes immediately after them must be thrown into the Accusative case. Here are the three most important prepositions in the Greek language: A. The word "Σε" (In / At / To) The word σε is the ultimate location word. It means in , at , or to , depending on the context of the sentence! What happens when σε meets a definite article (τον, την, το)? They smash together into one word! σε + τον = στον (to the / in the - masculine) σε + την = στην (to the / in the - feminine) σε + το = στο (to the / in the - neuter) English : I am going to the cinema. (cinema = το σινεμά) Greek : Πάω στο σινεμά. English : I work at the bank. (bank = η τράπεζα) Greek : Δουλεύω στην τράπεζα. B. The word "Από" (From) The word από means "from". It is used for origins and starting points. Unlike σε , από does NOT smash together with the article. They sit next to each other normally, but the noun is still in the Accusative! English : I am from Greece. (Greece = η Ελλάδα) Greek : Είμαι από την Ελλάδα. English : I am coming from the doctor. (doctor = ο γιατρός) Greek : Έρχομαι από τον γιατρό. (Notice that "γιατρός" dropped its -ς!) C. The word "Με" (With) The word με means "with". English : I am with Maria. Greek : Είμαι με τη Μαρία. English : I want coffee with sugar. (sugar = η ζάχαρη) Greek : Θέλω καφέ με ζάχαρη. (Notice we didn't use an article here because we don't mean "the specific sugar"!) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that Action verbs (like 'I see', 'I read') force the following noun into the Accusative ( τον/την/το ). [ ] I know that the 'mirror' verb είμαι keeps nouns in the Nominative ( ο/η/το ). [ ] I know that σε + τον = στον . [ ] I know that prepositions like σε, από, με always trigger the Accusative case on the word that follows them.

adjectives.title: Unit 6: Adjectives & Agreement
adjectives.content: The Adjectives (Τα Επίθετα) In English, adjectives are lazy. If you want to describe a man, a woman, or a child as "good," the word "good" never changes. - The good man. - The good woman. - The good child. In Greek, adjectives are hardworking. They must perfectly match the noun they are describing in gender, number, and case . We call this Agreement . 1. The Chameleon Words Because an adjective has to be ready to describe a masculine, feminine, or neuter noun, every single adjective in the dictionary comes in three versions . You can think of adjectives as chameleons that change their skin to match whatever noun they are standing next to. Let's look at the word for "good" ( καλός ): Masculine Version (for -ος nouns) Ο καλ ός πατέρας (The good father) Feminine Version (for -η nouns) Η καλ ή μητέρα (The good mother) Neuter Version (for -ο nouns) Το καλ ό παιδί (The good child) Notice how the ending of the adjective perfectly mirrors the ending of the noun? This is the beauty of Greek! Words rhyme with each other. [!TIP] Dictionary Format When you look up an adjective in a Greek dictionary, it will always be listed in the masculine form ( καλός ). It is up to you to change the ending to feminine ( καλή ) or neuter ( καλό ) if the noun requires it! 2. Common Adjective Families Almost all beginners adjectives fall into one of two spelling families: the -ος, -η, -ο family, or the -ος, -α, -ο family. Family A: The "-η" Adjectives These use an -η for the feminine version. Root Masculine Feminine Neuter Good καλ ός καλ ή καλ ό Tall ψηλ ός ψηλ ή ψηλ ό Small μικρ ός μικρ ή μικρ ό Family B: The "-α" Adjectives These use an -α for the feminine version. (Usually adjectives that have a vowel or a ρ right before the ending). Root Masculine Feminine Neuter Beautiful ωραί ος ωραί α ωραί ο New νέ ος νέ α νέ ο Cold κρύ ος κρύ α κρύ ο 3. Case Agreement (The Flexing Adjective) Remember Declension? Nouns change their endings when they become the Object (Accusative). Because adjectives are chameleons, they must change their endings too! If the noun flexes its grammatical muscle, the adjective has to flex with it. Let's look at "The good man" (masculine). As the Subject (Nom): Ο καλός άντρας είναι εδώ. (The good man is here). As the Object (Acc): Βλέπω τον καλό άντρα. (I see the good man). Notice what happened? The noun άντρας dropped its -ς because it became the object. Because the adjective καλός is attached to it, it also had to drop its -ς and become καλό ! [!NOTE] Does this happen to feminine and neuter words? No! Remember from the declension tables: feminine and neuter words never change their endings in the singular when becoming the object. Therefore, their adjectives don't change either! - Nom: Η καλή μητέρα - Acc: Βλέπω την καλή μητέρα 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I understand that every Greek adjective has three versions (masculine, feminine, neuter). [ ] I know that the adjective must perfectly match the gender and case of the noun it is attached to. [ ] I know that when a masculine noun drops its -ς in the object case, the adjective does exactly the same thing.

adverbs.title: adverbs
adverbs.content: The Adverbs Adverbs (Επιρρήματα) are indeclinable words that provide more information about verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. They tell us when , where , how , or how much something happened. Time (Χρονικά) σήμερα today αύριο tomorrow χτες yesterday τώρα now πάντα always ποτέ never Place (Τοπικά) εδώ here εκεί there πάνω up / on top κάτω down / below μέσα inside έξω outside Manner (Τροπικά) σιγά slowly / quietly γρήγορα quickly μαζί together έτσι like this ευτυχώς fortunately δυστυχώς unfortunately Quantity (Ποσοτικά) πολύ very / much λίγο a little αρκετά enough / quite καθόλου not at all Spelling Alert: πότε vs ποτέ Be careful with the accent! πότε (accent on the first syllable) means "when?" ποτέ (accent on the last syllable) means "never".

ancient-remnants.title: Unit 33: Ancient Remnants in Modern Greek
ancient-remnants.content: Ancient Remnants in Modern Greek Welcome to Level C2: Mastery . Modern Greek is a living, breathing language, but it carries the ghosts of its 3,000-year history everywhere it goes. In the 19th and 20th centuries, Greece had two official languages: 1. Demotic (Δημοτική): The natural spoken language of the people. 2. Katharevousa (Καθαρεύουσα): An artificial, highly purified, "ancient-sounding" language used by the government, schools, and news anchors. In 1976, Demotic officially won and became the sole language of Greece. However, the 150 years of Katharevousa left deep scars on the modern language. To achieve true C2 fluency, you must understand the "Ancient Remnants" that are still used in the news, literature, and formal speech today. 1. Dative Expressions (The Lost Case) In Level A, you learned the 4 modern Greek cases (Nominative, Genitive, Accusative, Vocative). Ancient Greek had a 5th case: The Dative Case (used to show "to" or "for" someone). Modern Greek replaced this entirely by using "σε + Accusative". However, dozens of Dative phrases survived as frozen, highly formal expressions. You cannot learn the grammar for these; you must simply memorize them as vocabulary. They are characterized by the suffix -ω (with an iota subscript that we don't write anymore) or -οις / -αις in the plural. Εν τω μεταξύ (In the meantime) Δόξα τω Θεώ (Glory to God / Thank God) Εν πάση περιπτώσει (In any case / Anyway) Εν τέλει (In the end / Finally) Τοις μετρητοίς (In cash) (Notice the tiny word εν (in). It is an ancient preposition that ALWAYS triggers the Dative case, and you will see it constantly in C2 vocabulary!) 2. Ancient Prepositions In Unit 21, we learned that most modern prepositions use the Accusative Case (σε, από, με) and some use the Genitive (υπέρ, κατά). C2 Greek resurrects several ancient prepositions that demand the Genitive case. They are used in formal contexts, journalism, and academic writing. Ancient Preposition Meaning Modern Equivalent Example Ενώπιον (+ Gen) Before / In the presence of Μπροστά σε Ορκίστηκε ενώπιον του δικαστηρίου. (He swore before the court). Λόγω (+ Gen) Due to / Because of Επειδή Κλειστό λόγω βροχής. (Closed due to rain). Μέσω (+ Gen) Via / Through Από Έφτασα μέσω Αθηνών. (I arrived via Athens). Άνευ (+ Gen) Without Χωρίς Προσοχή! Διασταύρωση άνευ σηματοδότη. (Caution! Junction without a traffic light). 3. The 3rd Declension Nouns (-ις / -εως) In Level A1, you learned the standard noun endings (-ος, -α, -η, -ι, -ο). However, Katharevousa dragged a group of ancient "3rd Declension" nouns into modern Greek. These are almost always abstract feminine nouns ending in -ις or -η (with ancient roots). Their defining feature is their weird Genitive singular ending: -εως . Η πόλις / Η πόλη (The city) ➔ Το κέντρο της πόλεως (The center of the city). Η κρίσις / Η κρίση (The crisis) ➔ Η λύση της κρίσεως (The solution to the crisis). Η κυβέρνησις / Η κυβέρνηση (The government) ➔ Ο εκπρόσωπος της κυβερνήσεως (The government spokesperson). (While it is perfectly fine to say "της πόλης" in everyday street Greek, any news anchor, politician, or formal writer will proudly say "της πόλεως" instead!) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I understand that Modern Greek contains formal remnants from Katharevousa and Ancient Greek. [ ] I have memorized common frozen Dative expressions like Εν τω μεταξύ (In the meantime) and Δόξα τω Θεώ (Thank God). [ ] I know that words like Λόγω (due to) and Μέσω (via) are formal prepositions that take the Genitive case. [ ] I recognize the formal feminine Genitive ending -εως (της πόλεως).

aorist-past.title: Unit 16: The Aorist (Simple Past)
aorist-past.chapter: 3. B1 The Great Divide: Verbal Aspect
aorist-past.content: The Aorist (Ο Αόριστος) The Aorist ( a-ó-ris-tos ) is the Simple Past Tense . It is the opposite of the Continuous Past (Imperfect). You use the Simple Past for events that: 1. Happened suddenly. 2. Happened once. 3. Have a clear beginning and end. Because we are taking a "polaroid snapshot" of a completed action, we must use the Simple Stem . 1. Creating the Simple Stem How do you turn a continuous verb like "παίζ-ω" (I play) into a simple snapshot? You change the final consonant of the stem! Greek verbs fall into three major families based on how their stems change. Family 1: The Sizzlers (-ζω, -νω, -θω) If the verb ends in -ζω , -νω , or -θω , the final consonant transforms into an "S" sound (Σ) . - παί ζ ω (I play) ➔ παί σ ... wait, that looks wrong. Because παίσ-α is so hard to say, the Greek language invented a special letter to make the [ps] sound easier: Ξ (Xi). Let's see how that looks in the table below! Wait, actually, verbs in -ζω go to -σ- (if they don't have a 'γ' hidden inside) or -ξ-. Let's make this much easier. Here are the true "cheat codes" for the vast majority of Type A verbs: Cheat Code 1: The 'Σ' Group Most verbs ending in -νω, -ζω, -θω change their stem to -σ . - διαβάζω (I read) ➔ διαβάσ- - κλείνω (I close) ➔ κλείσ- - νιώθω (I feel) ➔ νιώσ- Cheat Code 2: The 'Ξ' Group If the verb ends in a "throat" sound ( -κω, -γω, -χω ), that sound crashes into the 's' ending and explodes into the letter Ξ (xi) . - ανοίγω (I open) ➔ ανοίξ- - τρέχω (I run) ➔ τρέξ- - παίζω (I play) ➔ παίξ- (Yes, "παίζω" is officially an exception that joins the 'ξ' group!) Cheat Code 3: The 'Ψ' Group If the verb ends in a "lip" sound ( -πω, -βω, -φω ), the sound crashes into the 's' ending and explodes into the letter Ψ (psi) . - βλέπω (I see) ➔ This one is actually completely irregular! Hold on tight! - γράφω (I write) ➔ γράψ- - κρύβω (I hide) ➔ κρύψ- 2. The Simple Past Endings Remember the Past Tense endings from Unit 12 (The Imperfect)? Good news! The Aorist uses the exact same endings: -α, -ες, -ε, -αμε, -ατε, -αν(ε) Remember the Augment rule? Short verbs need an έ- added to the front to push the accent to the third-to-last syllable. Let's put it all together using the verb γράφω (I write) ➔ γράψ- . Pronoun Aorist Form English (Simple Past) (Εγώ) έ -γραψ- α I wrote (Εσύ) έ -γραψ- ες You wrote (Αυτό/ή/ό) έ -γραψ- ε He/She/It wrote (Εμείς) γράψ- αμε * We wrote (Εσείς) γράψ- ατε * You wrote (Αυτοί/ές/ά) έ -γραψ- αν They wrote (Notice how the Augment drops off in the 'We' and 'You' forms because the word is long enough!) 3. The Irregulars Greek is an ancient language, and many of the most common verbs are horribly irregular in the Past Tense. Their stems completely shatter and change into totally different words. You just have to bite the bullet and memorize these five absolute lifesavers: βλέπω (I see) ➔ είδα (I saw) λέω (I say) ➔ είπα (I said) πάω / πηγαίνω (I go) ➔ πήγα (I went) έχω (I have) ➔ είχα (I had) (Wait, "to have" doesn't have a simple past! You just use είχα for everything!) είμαι (I am) ➔ ήμουν (I was) Χθες, πήγα στο σινεμά και είδα μια ταινία. (Yesterday, I went to the cinema and I saw a movie.) Both are one-time, completed actions! So we use the Simple Past (Aorist) forms. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I understand that the Simple Past acts like a "Polaroid snapshot" of a completed action. [ ] I know how to turn a Continuous Stem into a Simple Stem by changing the final consonant to Σ, Ξ, or Ψ . [ ] I know that the endings are identical to the Imperfect Past ( -α, -ες, -ε... ). [ ] I have memorized the five crazy irregulars ( είδα, είπα, πήγα, είχα, ήμουν ).

articles-declension.title: articles-declension
articles-declension.content: Articles Declension (Η Κλίση των Άρθρων) You've made it to the final lesson of A1 Unit 2! Over the last few lessons, you have learned how masculine, feminine, and neuter nouns change shape between being the Subject (Nominative) and the Object (Accusative). To wrap up this unit, we are going to look at the Definite Articles ("The") all in one place. If you memorize this table, you will be able to guess the gender and case of almost any noun you see, even if you don't know what the word means! 1. Singular Articles (Ενικός αριθμός) Case/Role Masculine (He) Feminine (She) Neuter (It) Nom (Subject) ο η το Acc (Object) το(ν) τη(ν) το Gen (Owner) του της του Notice the patterns: 1. The Neuter is lazy: The neuter article το never changes between being the subject or the object. 2. The "T" Rule for Objects: In the Accusative case (when receiving the action), every single article starts with a Τ (το ν , τ ην, τ ο). If you see an article starting with T, it's probably the object! 2. Plural Articles (Πληθυντικός αριθμός) Now let's look at what happens when there is more than one item. Case/Role Masculine (They-m) Feminine (They-f) Neuter (They-n) Nom (Subject) οι οι τα Acc (Object) τους τις τα Gen (Owner) των των των Notice the patterns: 1. The Shared "οι": If you are talking about multiple men or multiple women who are doing an action (Subjects/Nominative), the article is exactly the same: οι . (e.g., Οι άντρες and Οι γυναίκες ). 2. The Shared "των": If you are talking about the owners of something (Genitive), the article is των for literally everything in the Greek language. It never changes. 3. The Neuter is still lazy: The plural neuter article τα never changes between being the subject or the object. 🎯 A1 Unit 2 Summary Congratulations! You now understand the core engine of the Greek language: Declensions . If you can remember the following three laws, you are ready to start conjugating verbs in Unit 3: - [ ] Law 1: Nouns change their endings based on whether they are the "Doer" (Nominative) or the "Receiver" (Accusative). - [ ] Law 2: You must always match the article ( ο, η, το , etc.) to the noun's gender and case. - [ ] Law 3: Almost all Masculine words drop their final -ς when they become the Object (Accusative). Feminine and Neuter words do not change.

articles.title: articles
articles.content: The Articles (Τα Άρθρα) In Greek, almost every noun must be preceded by an article (the little words for "the" or "a/an"). You cannot ignore them. If you drop the article in a Greek sentence, you will sound like a caveman ("Me go store. Buy apple.") The article is the anchor of the sentence. It tells the listener the gender (male/female/neuter) and the number (singular/plural) of the noun that is about to follow. 1. The Definite Article ("The") We use the definite article when we are talking about a specific thing (e.g., "The car is red"). In English, we only have one word for this: The . In Greek, "The" shape-shifts based on the gender of the noun. You already learned these in the previous lesson! Gender "The" (Singular) Example Translation Masculine ο ο δρόμος (o dró-mos) the street Feminine η η γυναίκα (ee yi-né-ka) the woman Neuter το το παιδί (to pe-dí) the child [!CAUTION] Names Take Articles! In English, we never put "The" in front of someone's name. You don't say "The Maria is here." In Greek, you absolutely must. - Ο Γιώργος είναι εδώ. (George is here). - Η Μαρία είναι εδώ. (Maria is here). 2. The Indefinite Article ("A / An") We use the indefinite article when we are talking about a non-specific thing (e.g., "I want a car"). Just like "The", the Greek word for "A" changes based on the gender. Gender "A / An" Example Translation Masculine ένας ένας δρόμος (é-nas dró-mos) a street Feminine μία / μια μια γυναίκα (mya yi-né-ka) a woman Neuter ένα ένα παιδί (é-na pe-dí) a child Notice how the indefinite articles share the same endings as the nouns they describe? ένας ends in -ας (masculine), μία ends in -α (feminine), and ένα ends in -α (neuter rule-breaker, but very easy to remember). [!TIP] μία vs. μια You will see the feminine article spelled both ways. - Μία (mí-a) has two distinct syllables. It is slightly more formal and is also used as the number "1". - Μια (mya) is a single syllable. It is used in fast, casual speech. They mean exactly the same thing. Example Sentences Let's look at how replacing "The" with "A" changes the meaning exactly as it does in English. Ο φίλος είναι εδώ. ➔ The (specific) friend is here. Ένας φίλος είναι εδώ. ➔ A (random) friend is here. Θέλω το βιβλίο. ➔ I want the (specific) book. Θέλω ένα βιβλίο. ➔ I want a (random) book. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I will literally never say a Greek noun or a person's name without putting "The" ( ο, η, το ) or "A" ( ένας, μία, ένα ) in front of it. [ ] I know that "μία" and "μια" are the exact same word.

aspect-intro.title: Unit 15: Introduction to Aspect
aspect-intro.chapter: 3. B1 The Great Divide: Verbal Aspect
aspect-intro.content: Introduction to Verbal Aspect (Το Ποιόν Ενέργειας) Welcome to Level B1. You have reached the Great Divide in the Greek language. If there is one concept that separates beginners from fluent speakers, it is Verbal Aspect (Το Ποιόν Ενέργειας). Everything you have learned so far—the Present tense, the Subjunctive, the Continuous Future, the Imperfect Past—has used the exact same stem of the verb (the Present Stem ). Now, it is time to learn the second stem. 1. What is Aspect? In English, we change the timeline of a verb by adding words. - I play . (Present) - I will play . (Future) - I played . (Past) This tells us when the action happened. This is called Tense . Greek also has Tense. But Greek cares just as much about how the action happened. This is called Aspect . Greek forces you to decide between two "Hows" every time you speak: 1. The Continuous Aspect: Is the action a repeated habit, an ongoing state, or something in the background? 2. The Simple Aspect: Is the action a one-time event, a completed snapshot, or a sudden burst? [!TIP] Think of it like taking a video vs. taking a photo. - Continuous Aspect is a video camera. It shows the action happening over a period of time. - Simple Aspect is a polaroid camera. It takes a single, instant snapshot of a completed action. 2. The Two Stems Because Greek hates ambiguity, it doesn't just rely on context to tell you if an action is a "video" or a "photo". It literally gives you two different verb stems. Every single active verb you learn from now on has two forms you must memorize: 1. The Present Stem (The Video Camera). Used for Continuous actions. 2. The Aorist Stem (The Polaroid). Used for Simple/Completed actions. Let's look at our favorite verb: παίζω (I play). - Continuous Stem: παίζ- (The 'Z' sound) - Simple Stem: παίξ- (The 'X' sound) Scenario A: The Continuous Future (Video) "I will play tennis every day." Because this is a repeated habit, we must use the Continuous stem. ➔ Θα παίζ ω τένις κάθε μέρα. Scenario B: The Simple Future (Photo) "I will play tennis tomorrow at 5 PM." Because this is a single, one-time event that will start and finish, we must use the Simple stem. ➔ Θα παίξ ω τένις αύριο. Both sentences mean "I will play". But changing that single letter from a 'ζ' to an 'ξ' changes the entire meaning of the sentence from a lifelong habit to a one-off appointment! 3. The Grand Matrix You use these two stems to build almost all the tenses you need in Greek. Tense / Mood Which stem do I use? Example (παίζω) Present Continuous (It's happening right now) Παίζω τώρα. Imperfect Past Continuous (I used to play) Έπαιζα κάθε μέρα. Simple Past (Aorist) Simple (I played once ) Έπαιξα χθες. Continuous Future Continuous (I will be playing) Θα παίζω πάντα. Simple Future Simple (I will play once) Θα παίξω αύριο. Continuous Subjunctive Continuous (I want to be playing ) Θέλω να παίζω . Simple Subjunctive Simple (I want to play a game ) Θέλω να παίξω . Over the next few lessons, we are going to learn exactly how to build the Simple Stem (the Aorist Stem) so you can take "photos" of your actions in the Past, the Future, and the Subjunctive! 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I understand that Tense tells me when an action happens, and Aspect tells me how it happens. [ ] I know that the Continuous stem (Video) is for habits, routines, and ongoing actions. [ ] I know that the Simple stem (Photo) is for sudden, single, or completed actions.

auxiliary-verbs.title: auxiliary-verbs
auxiliary-verbs.content: Auxiliary Verbs Auxiliary verbs are "helper" verbs used to form complex tenses. The two most important auxiliary verbs in Greek are είμαι (to be) and έχω (to have). 1. To Be (Είμαι) Person Present (I am) Past (I was) Εγώ είμαι ήμουν(α) Εσύ είσαι ήσουν(α) Αυτός/ή/ό είναι ήταν(ε) Εμείς είμαστε ήμασταν Εσείς είστε ήσασταν Αυτοί/ές/ά είναι ήταν(ε) 2. To Have (Έχω) Person Present (I have) Past (I had) Εγώ έχω είχα Εσύ έχεις είχες Αυτός/ή/ό έχει είχε Εμείς έχουμε είχαμε Εσείς έχετε είχατε Αυτοί/ές/ά έχουν(ε) είχαν(ε) Did you know? The verb έχω is used exactly like in English to form the perfect tenses. Example: Έχω διαβάσει. (I have read).

basics.title: Unit 1: The Greek Alphabet
basics.content: The Greek Alphabet Welcome to your first step toward mastering Greek! As an English speaker, learning the Greek alphabet is much easier than you might think. Did you know that the word "alphabet" itself comes from the first two Greek letters: Alpha (Α) and Beta (Β) ? Over 25% of English vocabulary has Greek roots, meaning you already know a lot of Greek without even realizing it. The Greek alphabet consists of 24 letters . We will break them down into three easy-to-digest groups based on how similar they are to English. Group 1: The "Freebies" These letters look identical to their English counterparts and make the exact same sounds. You already know these: Letter (Cap & Lower) Name Pronunciation Example in English Α α Alpha [a] as in f a ther A pple Ε ε Epsilon [e] as in p e t E lephant Ι ι Iota [ee] as in m ee t Ignorant (but purely ee sound) Κ κ Kappa [k] as in k i te K ing Μ μ Mu [m] as in m o m M other Ν ν Nu [n] as in n o N ever Ο ο Omicron [o] as in t o p O live Τ τ Tau [t] as in t o p T ime Ζ ζ Zeta [z] as in z oo Z ebra Group 2: The "False Friends" These letters look like English letters but make a completely different sound . Watch out for these—they are the most common source of early mistakes for English speakers! Letter Name Pronunciation & Warning Example Β β Beta Sounds like an English V (like in v a n ). Do not pronounce it as 'B'! V ideo Η η Eta Sounds exactly like Ι (Iota) : [ee] as in m ee t . It is not an 'H'. S ee Ρ ρ Rho Sounds like a lightly rolled R . It is not a 'P'. R oll Χ χ Chi A throaty 'H' sound, like the Scottish lo ch or Spanish J . It is not an 'X'. Lo ch Υ υ Upsilon Another [ee] sound! It sounds identical to Ι and Η . It is not a 'Y' or 'U'. Fr ee [!TIP] Why are there three letters that sound like "ee"? In Ancient Greek, Ι , Η , and Υ represented different sounds (short i, long e, and a French u). Over thousands of years, pronunciation shifted, and today they all make the exact same [ee] sound! This is called "Iotacism." You will learn when to use which purely through spelling rules later on. Group 3: The "Aliens" These letters look entirely new, but their sounds are very straightforward. Letter Name Pronunciation Example in English Γ γ Gamma A soft, guttural G, or sometimes a 'Y'. Roughly like the y in y es . Y oghurt / Spanish a g ua Δ δ Delta A soft th sound, like in th i s or th a t . Th ere Θ θ Theta A hard, breathy th sound, like in th i nk or th e me . Th eory Λ λ Lambda An L sound, like in l i on . L ist Ξ ξ Xi A ks sound, like the English 'x' in bo x . Ta x i Π π Pi A P sound, like in p e n . P aper Σ σ, ς Sigma An S sound, like in s u n . S top Φ φ Phi An F sound, like in f i sh . F ly Ψ ψ Psi A ps sound, like in o ps ! Ste ps Ω ω Omega An O sound. Sounds exactly like Ο (Omicron) . L o w [!CAUTION] The Two Faces of Sigma Notice that Sigma has two lowercase forms: σ and ς . Use the round σ anywhere inside a word: σύστημα (system). Use the final ς only if it is the very last letter of a word: οδός (street). 🎯 Quick Review & Orthographic Notes Capitalization : Greek uses uppercase letters exactly like English—for names of people, places, and at the beginning of sentences. (e.g., Αθήνα = Athens). Punctuation : The Greek question mark looks exactly like an English semicolon ( ; ). The English question mark (?) is never used! The "ee" and "o" sounds : - [ee] can be spelled ι , η , or υ . - [o] can be spelled ο or ω . (There is zero difference in pronunciation today. Don't stress about it; you will pick up the spelling patterns naturally). Take your time familiarizing yourself with these letters. In the next lesson, we will look at what happens when you combine two letters together to make completely new sounds!

cases.title: cases
cases.content: The Cases (Οι Πτώσεις) Welcome to the feature of Greek grammar that terrifies English speakers the most: The Cases . Take a deep breath. Cases are actually very logical. They are just a way of tagging words so you know what job they are doing in a sentence. Think of a sentence as a movie set. You have: - The Director (The Subject doing the action) - The Actor (The Direct Object receiving the action) - The Producer (The person who owns something) In English, we know who is who based on word order . "The dog ate the cat" means something very different than "The cat ate the dog." In Greek, you can put the words in any order you want! How do you know who is the eater and who is the eaten? You tag them with Cases . 1. Nominative (Ονομαστική): The Subject The Nominative Case is the dictionary form of a word. You use it when the noun is the Subject of the sentence (the one doing the action). Question to ask: "Who or what is doing the action?" Ο Γιώργος πίνει καφέ. (George drinks coffee.) George is the one doing the drinking. He is in the Nominative case. Η Μαρία είναι εδώ. (Maria is here.) Maria is the subject. She is in the Nominative case. Every noun you have learned so far (ο φίλος, η μητέρα, το βιβλίο) has been in the Nominative case. 2. Accusative (Αιτιατική): The Object The Accusative Case is used when the noun is the Direct Object of the sentence (the one receiving the action). Question to ask: "Who or what is being acted upon?" Ο Γιώργος βλέπει τη Μαρία . (George sees Maria.) George is looking. Maria is being looked at. So, Maria changes to the Accusative form ( τη Μαρία ). This is exactly like the difference between "I" and "Me" or "He" and "Him" in English! - He sees her. (Nominative) - She sees him . (Accusative) Greek just takes this concept and applies it to every single noun in the language , not just pronouns. 3. Genitive (Γενική): Possession The Genitive Case is used to show ownership or relationship. It replaces the English word "of" or the apostrophe-'s (John's car). Question to ask: "Whose is it?" Το βιβλίο της Μαρίας . (The book of Maria / Maria's book.) 4. Vocative (Κλητική): Addressing someone The Vocative Case is used exclusively when you are calling out to someone or talking directly to them. Question to ask: "Hey!" Γεια σου, Γιώργο ! (Hello, George!) Summary of Case Tags Watch how poor George's name changes depending on his role in the movie: Nominative (Subject) : Ο Γιώργος is reading a book. Accusative (Object) : I see τον Γιώργο . Genitive (Owner) : This is the car of του Γιώργου . Vocative (Calling) : Hey Γιώργο , come here! For the rest of the A1 level, we are only going to focus on the first two: Nominative (Subject) and Accusative (Object). Master those, and you can say almost anything! 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I understand that Greek words change their endings to show their "job" in the sentence. [ ] I know the Nominative case is for the "Doer" (Subject). [ ] I know the Accusative case is for the "Receiver" (Object).

colors-numbers.title: colors-numbers
colors-numbers.content: Colors and Numbers (Χρώματα και Αριθμοί) Adjectives are like chameleons: they change their endings to match the noun they are describing. What better way to practice this than with Colors and Numbers? 1. Colors (Τα Χρώματα) Most colors in Greek act exactly like the adjectives you just learned. They follow the -ος, -η, -ο or -ος, -α, -ο patterns. English Masculine Feminine Neuter Red κόκκιν ος κόκκιν η κόκκιν ο Black μαύρ ος μαύρ η μαύρ ο Green πράσιν ος πράσιν η πράσιν ο White άσπρ ος άσπρ η άσπρ ο Yellow κίτριν ος κίτριν η κίτριν ο Grey γκρίζ ος γκρίζ α γκρίζ ο The "Undeclinable" Colors However, some colors were borrowed from foreign languages (like French or Italian). Remember what we learned about foreign nouns like το ίντερνετ ? They are stubborn and refuse to change their endings. These colors never change . They look exactly the same whether they describe a man or a woman: - Μπλε (ble) = Blue - Ροζ (roz) = Pink - Καφέ (ka-fé) = Brown Ο μπλε ουρανός. (The blue sky - Masculine) Η μπλε θάλασσα. (The blue sea - Feminine) Το μπλε αυτοκίνητο. (The blue car - Neuter) 2. Numbers 1 to 10 (Αριθμοί 1-10) Like English, numbers in Greek are incredibly important. Here are the first ten numbers. Ένα (é-na) Δύο (dí-o) Τρία (trí-a) Τέσσερα (tés-se-ra) Πέντε (pén-te) Έξι (é-xi) Επτά (ep-tá) Οκτώ (oc-tó) Εννέα (en-né-a) Δέκα (dé-ka) Note: You will often hear Greeks say "εφτά", "οχτώ", and "εννιά" instead of the formal spellings above. Both are totally acceptable! The Gender Test Numbers are generally rigid. The number 5 is always πέντε , no matter what you are counting. But there are exceptions. Remember how the number '1' was the same as the indefinite article ('A')? Because the number 1 acts like an article, it has to change its gender to match the noun it is counting! Do you remember the table from Unit 2? Ένας άντρας (One man) Μία γυναίκα (One woman) Ένα παιδί (One child) This rule also applies to the numbers 3 ( Τρεις / Τρία ) and 4 ( Τέσσερις / Τέσσερα ), but you don't need to memorize those forms until level A2. For now, just remember that the number 1 is a chameleon! 3. Practice Sentences Let's put everything we've learned together! See if you can spot the Accusative Case (Direct Objects) and the Adjective Agreements working together. Ο Γιώργος έχει ένα κόκκινο αυτοκίνητο. (George has a red car.) Notice that το became ένα , and κόκκινο ends in -ο to match αυτοκίνητο . Η Μαρία βλέπει τον μαύρο σκύλο. (Maria sees the black dog.) Notice that ο σκύλος became τον σκύλο because of the Accusative rule, and μαύρος had to follow along and drop its -s! 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that most colors change their endings to match the noun ( κόκκινος, κόκκινη, κόκκινο ). [ ] I know that foreign colors like μπλε and ροζ never change. [ ] I know how to count to 10. [ ] I know that the number 1 changes gender ( ένας, μία, ένα ) depending on what is being counted.
