compound-words.title: Unit 32: Compound Words
compound-words.content: Compound Words (Σύνθετες Λέξεις) Greek vocabulary is massive, but it's incredibly logical. Instead of inventing brand new words for every concept, Greeks build words like Lego bricks by snapping smaller words together. Once you understand how the bricks connect, you can usually guess the meaning of a C1/C2 word even if you've never seen it before! These Lego-block words are called Compound Words . 1. The Greek "Glue": The letter Omikron (ο) When you attach two Greek words together, you almost always need "glue" to connect them. In Greek, the glue is the letter ο (omikron). If the first word ends in a normal vowel (like -ος or -η), that vowel simply turns into an ο . Then the second word attaches to it! Example 1: The Bookstore βιβλίο (book) + πωλώ (I sell) βιβλίο ➔ βιβλι- + glue ο ➔ βιβλι ο - βιβλι ο - + -πωλείο (selling place) = βιβλιοπωλείο (bookstore). Example 2: The Fish Tavern ψάρι (fish) + ταβέρνα (tavern) ψάρι ➔ ψαρ- + glue ο ➔ ψαρ ο - ψαρ ο - + ταβέρνα = ψαροταβέρνα (fish tavern). 2. Prefixes (The Power Bricks) There are dozens of highly active prefixes (mini-words) that you can slap onto the front of any word to instantly completely change its meaning. Ξανα- (Again / Re-) If you want to say "do it again" or "re-do", just add ξανα- to the verb! - κάνω (I do) ➔ ξανα κάνω (I re-do) - βλέπω (I see) ➔ ξανα βλέπω (I re-watch / see again) - λέω (I say) ➔ ξανα λέω (I repeat) Α- / Αν- (The Negative / Un-) This is the famous Alpha Privative. In English, we use "un-" or "in-" (unhappy, invisible). Greek uses a simple α- (if the next letter is a consonant) or αν- (if the next letter is a vowel). - γνωστός (known) ➔ ά γνωστος (unknown) - δίκαιος (fair/just) ➔ ά δικος (unfair) - ικανός (capable) ➔ αν ίκανος (incapable) - ηθικός (ethical) ➔ αν ήθικος (unethical) Ξε- (The Reverser / Un-) This is the action reverser. While the Alpha Privative makes adjectives negative, ξε- is used to reverse the action of a verb (like tying and un-tying). - κλειδώνω (I lock) ➔ ξε κλειδώνω (I unlock) - δένω (I tie) ➔ ξε δένω (I untie) - βάφω (I paint) ➔ ξε βάφω (I un-paint / strip paint) 3. Creating Adjectives from Nouns You can stick two nouns together to create a powerful descriptive adjective. Always remember to use the glue ο ! χρώμα (color) + ασήμι (silver) = ασημόχρωμος (silver-colored). καρδιά (heart) + πέτρα (stone) = πετρόκαρδος (stone-hearted). κεφάλι (head) + ελαφρύ (light) = ελαφροκέφαλος (light-headed). 4. Famous Greek Roots Every Learner Must Know Once you hit C1, you will see these roots everywhere: -φίλος (Lover / Friend): - ζώο (animal) + φίλος = ζωόφιλος (animal lover). - ξένος (foreigner) + φίλος = φιλόξενος (hospitable). -φόβος (Fearer / Hater): - ξένος + φόβος = ξενοφοβία (fear of foreigners). - ύψος (height) + φόβος = υψοφοβία (fear of heights). -λόγος (Speaker / Studier of): - ψυχή (soul) + λόγος = ψυχολόγος (psychologist). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that Greek builds words by snapping them together using the letter ο as "glue". [ ] I can reverse an action using ξε- ( ξεκλειδώνω ). [ ] I can do something repeatedly using ξανα- ( ξαναλέω ). [ ] I know that adding an α- or αν- negates an adjective ( γνωστός -> άγνωστος ).

conditionals-1-2.title: Unit 25: Conditional Sentences (Type 1 & 2)
conditionals-1-2.content: Conditional Sentences (Υποθετικοί Λόγοι - Types 1 & 2) Conditional sentences are "If X, then Y" sentences. If I have money, then I will buy a car. If I were rich, then I would buy a boat. In Greek, the word for "If" is Αν (An) or Εάν (E-án). They mean exactly the same thing, but Αν is much more common in spoken Greek. There are three types of conditionals, but we are only going to focus on the first two in Level B2. Type 1: The "Real" Conditional (It's possible) The Type 1 conditional is used when the "If X" part is a real possibility. It might happen in the future, or it might be a general truth. You use this when you are talking about facts, plans, or likely outcomes. The Formula If [Present or Future], then [Future, Present, or Command]. Notice that this is exactly the same as English! You just use normal verb tenses. English: If it is raining (Present), I will stay (Future) at home. Greek: Αν βρέχει, θα μείνω στο σπίτι. English: If you want (Present), come (Command) with me. Greek: Αν θέλεις, έλα μαζί μου. English: If you heat (Present) ice, it melts (Present). Greek: Αν ζεσταίνεις τον πάγο, λιώνει. Type 2: The "Unreal" Conditional (Dreaming / Hypothetical) The Type 2 conditional is used when the "If X" part is a dream, highly unlikely, or currently impossible. You use this when you are giving advice ("If I were you...") or daydreaming ("If I had a million dollars..."). In English, we have to invent weird grammar to make this work: "If I were rich, I would buy a boat." Greek handles this in a wonderfully logical way. To show that something is a "fake" dream scenario, Greek pushes both verbs back into the Continuous Past (Imperfect Tense) . The Formula If [Continuous Past], then [Θα + Continuous Past]. Look at how beautifully symmetrical that is! 1. The "If" half uses the Continuous Past tense (έπαιζα). 2. The "Then" half uses the word θα + the Continuous Past tense (θα έπαιζα). In Greek, θα + Past Tense = "Would". English: If I had money (Right now, but I don't), I would buy a car. Greek: Αν είχα λεφτά, θα αγόραζα ένα αυτοκίνητο. (Both verbs are in the Continuous Past tense!) English: If I were you, I would tell him. Greek: Αν ήμουν εσύ, θα του έλεγα . English: What would you do? Greek: Τι θα έκανες ; 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the word for "If" is Αν . [ ] I know that Type 1 Conditionals ("Real / Possible") just use standard Present and Future tenses. [ ] I know that Type 2 Conditionals ("Unreal / Dreaming") push both verbs into the Continuous Past tense. [ ] I know that Θα + Continuous Past Tense translates to "Would" in English ( θα πήγαινα = I would go).

conditionals-3.title: Unit 28: Conditional Sentences (Type 3)
conditionals-3.content: Conditional Sentences (Type 3 - The Past Unreal) In Level B2, you learned about the Type 2 Conditional (The Unreal Dream). - Type 2: If I had money (right now), I would buy a car (right now). - Greek: Αν είχα λεφτά, θα αγόραζα ένα αυτοκίνητο. But what if you are talking about regret? What if it's too late because everything already happened in the past? Type 3: If I had known (yesterday), I would have gone (yesterday). This is the Past Unreal Conditional . It is used to talk about things that never actually happened in the past, and what the alternative outcome would have been. 1. The English Headache vs The Greek Logic In English, making a Type 3 conditional is a grammatical nightmare. You need a mix of Past Perfect and Future Perfect in the Past: "If I had seen you, I would have said hello." In Greek, Type 3 is incredibly logical and easy to build. Do you remember the Past Perfect tense from Unit 23? That's when you take the past continuous helper verb είχα (I had) + the frozen simple stem [verb]-ει . - είχα δει (I had seen) - είχα πάει (I had gone) To make a Type 3 conditional in Greek, you just use the Past Perfect tense for both sides of the sentence! To show that the second half is a hypothetical "would have", you just put θα in front of it! The Formula If [Past Perfect], then [Θα + Past Perfect]. Let's look at the breakdown: English: If I had seen you, I would have spoken to you. Greek Step 1 (If): Αν είχα δει εσένα... Greek Step 2 (Then): ... θα είχα μιλήσει σε εσένα. Full Sentence: Αν σε είχα δει, θα σου είχα μιλήσει. English: If he had read the book, he would have passed the test. Greek: Αν είχε διαβάσει το βιβλίο, θα είχε περάσει τις εξετάσεις. 2. The Spoken Shortcut (The "Lazy" C1 Mix) While the formula above ( Είχα X ➔ Θα είχα Y ) is grammatically perfect and highly recommended for C1 level writing, native speakers are often lazy when they speak. Sometimes, in spoken Greek, people will mix Type 2 (Imperfect) and Type 3 (Past Perfect) together to talk about the past. For example, to say "If I had known, I wouldn't have come": Strict Type 3: Αν είχα ξέρει , δεν θα είχα έρθει . (Correct, but very long). Spoken Greek: Αν το ήξερα , δεν θα ερχόμουν . Wait, isn't "Αν το ήξερα" a Type 2 conditional (If I knew [right now])? Yes! But Greeks often use the Imperfect tense to refer to the Past Unreality because it's faster to say than the full Past Perfect. Context is everything. If the conversation is about a party that happened yesterday, "Αν το ήξερα" definitely means "If I had known (yesterday)". Recommended Usage for C1 Learners: Stick to the strict Type 3 grammar ( είχα + ει ➔ θα είχα + ει ). It is always elegant, always correct, and it proves you have mastered advanced Greek syntax! 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that Type 3 Conditionals are used for past regrets and things that never happened ("If I had known..."). [ ] I know that the formula uses the Past Perfect Tense on both sides of the comma. [ ] I know how to construct it: Αν είχα [πάει] ➔ θα είχα [μιλήσει]. [ ] I am aware that native speakers sometimes take shortcuts using the Imperfect tense in casual contexts.

conjugation-present.title: conjugation-present
conjugation-present.content: Present Conjugation (Type A) You now know that Type A verbs end in an unaccented -ω . How do we conjugate them? We take the Stem , and we add the Endings for the Present Tense. Here is the master table for Type A verbs in the Present Tense. We will use the verb διαβάζω (I read) as our example. Pronoun Verb Ending Pronunciation (Εγώ) διαβάζ ω -ω (di-a-vá-zo) (Εσύ) διαβάζ εις -εις (di-a-vá-zis) (Αυτό/ή/ό) διαβάζ ει -ει (di-a-vá-zi) (Εμείς) διαβάζ ουμε -ουμε (di-a-vá-zou-me) (Εσείς) διαβάζ ετε -ετε (di-a-vá-ze-te) (Αυτοί/ές/ά) διαβάζ ουν(ε) -ουν(ε) (di-a-vá-zoun / zoun-e) [!TIP] The Spelling Cheat Sheet Notice that the "You" and "He/She" endings ( -εις and -ει ) sound exactly the same: [is] and [i] . But they are always spelled with the ει combination! This is a strict rule. You will never see a Type A verb end in -ις or -ι . 2. Let's Practice! Let's take a brand new verb: γράφω (I write). Step 1: Find the stem by chopping off the -ω . The stem is γράφ- . Step 2: Add the endings from the table above. (I write) ➔ γράφω (You write) ➔ γράφεις (He/She writes) ➔ γράφει (We write) ➔ γράφουμε (You all write) ➔ γράφετε (They write) ➔ γράφουν 3. The "They" Ending: -ουν vs -ουνε You might have noticed the (ε) on the "They" ending: διαβάζουν(ε) . In Greek, you can add an extra -ε to the end of the 3rd person plural to make it flow better in speech. Τα παιδιά διαβάζ ουν το βιβλίο. (Correct) Τα παιδιά διαβάζ ουνε το βιβλίο. (Also correct, and very common!) There is zero difference in meaning. It's just a matter of rhythm. When you are writing formally, stick to -ουν . When you are speaking casually, you will hear -ουνε all the time. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I have memorized the six endings for Type A verbs: -ω, -εις, -ει, -ουμε, -ετε, -ουν. [ ] I can conjugate any Type A verb by finding its stem and adding these endings. [ ] I know that the "He/She" ending sounds like [ee] but is always spelled -ει .

conjunctions.title: conjunctions
conjunctions.content: The Conjunctions Conjunctions (Σύνδεσμοι) are words used to connect sentences, phrases, or individual words. They are essential for building complex thoughts and flowing conversation. Connecting (Συμπλεκτικοί) και / κι and ούτε / μήτε neither / nor ή ... ή either ... or Contrasting (Αντιθετικοί) αλλά / μα but όμως / ωστόσο however / yet αν και although ενώ while Causal (Αιτιολογικοί) γιατί because / why επειδή since / as διότι because (formal) Time (Χρονικοί) όταν when πριν before αφού after / since μόλις as soon as Result (Συμπερασματικοί) άρα / επομένως therefore λοιπόν so / well ώστε so that Purpose / Condition να / για να to / so that αν / εάν if μήπως maybe / lest Common Patterns Θέλω να πάω αλλά δεν μπορώ. (I want to go but I cannot.) Είναι καλό γιατί είναι φθηνό. (It is good because it is cheap.)

declension.title: declension
declension.content: The Declension (Η Κλίση) You now know that Greek nouns are tagged with Cases depending on what job they do in a sentence (Subject vs. Object). But how do we actually "tag" them? We change the ends of the words. This changing of word endings is a process called Declension (Η Κλίση). When a Greek noun changes its role in a sentence, three things actually shape-shift together to match the new role: 1. The Article (the word for "the" or "a") 2. The Noun itself 3. Any Adjectives attached to the noun The "Flexing" Language In English, nouns are incredibly stiff. The word "friend" looks the same whether they are doing something or having something done to them. The friend is here. I see the friend . In Greek, nouns are flexible. They "flex" their endings to show you their grammatical muscle. Look at what happens to the masculine word ο φίλος (the friend). Scenario A: The Subject (Nominative) The friend is doing the action. Ο φίλος είναι εδώ. (The friend is here.) Notice the -ος ending. This is his resting state. Scenario B: The Object (Accusative) The friend is receiving the action. Βλέπω τον φίλο . (I see the friend.) Notice what happened! 1. The article ο grew an 'n' and became τον . 2. The noun φίλος dropped its 's' and became φίλο . Why does Greek do this? It might seem like a lot of extra work just to say "I see the friend." But this flexibility gives Greek a superpower: Word Order Freedom . In English, if you scramble the words "The dog bit the man", the sentence completely breaks down or changes meaning ("The man bit the dog"). In Greek, because the actor ( Ο σκύλος ) and the victim ( Τον άντρα ) are clearly tagged with their case endings, you can arrange the sentence however you like! Ο σκύλος δάγκωσε τον άντρα . (Normal) Τον άντρα δάγκωσε ο σκύλος . (Poetic/Emphatic) Δάγκωσε ο σκύλος τον άντρα . (Dramatic) All three sentences mean exactly the same thing: The dog bit the man. The Good News You don't have to guess how words change. Greek nouns belong to very predictable families based on their gender and ending. In the next three lessons, we will look at the specific cheat codes (Declension Tables) for Masculine, Feminine, and Neuter nouns. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I understand that Greek words change their endings to signal their grammatical role. [ ] I know that the Article, the Noun, and the Adjective all change together. [ ] I realize this allows Greek sentences to be put in almost any order.

definite-pronouns.title: definite-pronouns
definite-pronouns.content: Definite Pronouns Definite pronouns in Greek are used to express identity ("the same") or exclusivity ("alone / by oneself"). ίδιος, ίδια, ίδιο The Same Always used with a definite article. Πήγα στον ίδιο γιατρό. I went to the same doctor.Έχουμε τα ίδια ρούχα. We have the same clothes. μόνος, μόνη, μόνο Alone / By oneself Often accompanied by a weak possessive pronoun (μου, σου...) to mean "by myself". Το τελείωσα μόνος μου . I finished it by myself . Vocabulary Note Be careful not to confuse the adjective μόνος (alone) with the adverb μόνο (only). Example: Ήμουν μόνος . (I was alone ) vs Έφαγα μόνο ένα. (I only ate one).

degrees-adjectives.title: Unit 20: Degrees of Comparison
degrees-adjectives.chapter: 3. B1 The Great Divide: Verbal Aspect
degrees-adjectives.content: Degrees of Comparison (Παραθετικά Επιθέτων) In Level A1, you learned how to use basic adjectives to describe things: "The tall man" (Ο ψηλός άντρας). But what if you want to say the man is taller than his friend? What if he is the tallest man in the world? Or what if he is just very tall ? These are called the "Degrees of Comparison". There are three levels: 1. Positive Degree : Tall. 2. Comparative Degree : Taller. 3. Superlative Degree : The tallest / Very tall. 1. The Comparative Degree (More / -er) In English, we say "more beautiful" (for long words) or "tall er " (for short words). Greek does exactly the same thing! There are two ways to form the comparative. Method A: The Easy Way (Using "πιο") The easiest and most common way to say "more" in Greek is to just put the tiny word πιο (pi-o) in front of the adjective. ωραίος (beautiful) ➔ πιο ωραίος (more beautiful) έξυπνος (smart) ➔ πιο έξυπνος (smarter) ψηλός (tall) ➔ πιο ψηλός (taller) (Note: πιο never changes. The adjective still has to match the noun like a chameleon! "Η γυναίκα είναι πιο ωραία ".) Method B: The Suffix (-ότερος) You can also add a suffix to the end of the word, just like the English "-er". Take the adjective, drop the 'ος', and add -ότερος, -ότερη, -ότερο . ωραίος ➔ ωραι ότερος (more beautiful) ψηλός ➔ ψηλ ότερος (taller) (Both methods are 100% correct, but Method A is much more common in spoken Greek). 2. Using "Than" (από) How do you use the Comparative in a sentence? You need the word "than". In Greek, the word for "than" is από (from/than). The noun that follows από will always be in the Accusative Case (the Object case). Ο Γιώργος είναι πιο ψηλός από τον Κώστα. (George is taller than Kostas.) (Literally: George is more tall from Kostas). Το σπίτι μου είναι μεγαλύτερο από το δικό σου. (My house is bigger than yours.) 3. The Superlative Degree (The Most / Very) The Superlative degree has two different flavors in Greek. Flavor 1: Relative Superlative (The tallest) If you want to say "the tallest in the group", you use the Comparative form, but you put the Definite Article (o, η, το) directly in front of it! πιο ψηλός (taller) ➔ ο πιο ψηλός (the tallest) ψηλότερος (taller) ➔ ο ψηλότερος (the tallest) Ο Γιώργος είναι ο πιο ψηλός στην τάξη. (George is the tallest in the class.) Flavor 2: Absolute Superlative (Very tall) If you just want to say someone is "very, very tall" (without comparing them to anyone specific), you can add the extreme suffix -ότατος, -ότατη, -ότατο . ψηλός (tall) ➔ ψηλ ότατος (extremely tall) ωραίος (beautiful) ➔ ωραι ότατη (exquisitely beautiful) (Pro-Tip: You can also just say πολύ [very] + normal adjective: πολύ ψηλός). 4. The 3 Crazy Irregulars There are three adjectives that are used so often, their comparative forms are almost completely irregular. You must memorize these: καλός (good) ➔ καλύτερος (better) ➔ ο καλύτερος (best) κακός (bad) ➔ χειρότερος (worse) ➔ ο χειρότερος (worst) μεγάλος (big/old) ➔ μεγαλύτερος (bigger/older) ➔ ο μεγαλύτερος (biggest/oldest) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know how to make an adjective Comparative ("more") by adding πιο or the suffix -ότερος . [ ] I know that the word for "than" is από + Accusative ( πιο ψηλός από τον Κώστα ). [ ] I know how to make an adjective Superlative ("the most") by adding an article before the comparative ( ο πιο ψηλός ). [ ] I have memorized the 3 irregulars ( καλύτερος, χειρότερος, μεγαλύτερος ).

demonstrative-pronouns.title: demonstrative-pronouns
demonstrative-pronouns.content: Demonstrative Pronouns Demonstrative pronouns are used to point to specific objects or people, indicating their relative distance from the speaker. αυτός, αυτή, αυτό This (Close by) Used for things near the speaker. Αυτός είναι ο καφές μου. This is my coffee. εκείνος, εκείνη, εκείνο That (Far away) Used for things distant from the speaker. Εκείνο το σπίτι. That house. τέτοιος, τέτοια, τέτοιο Such Used to indicate quality or type. Δεν είναι τέτοιος άνθρωπος. He is not such a man. τόσος, τόση, τόσο So much / many Used to indicate quantity or degree. Πήγαμε τόσο μακριά. We went so far. Grammatical Note When a demonstrative pronoun is used with a noun, the noun must also have an article . Example: Αυτός ο σκύλος (This [the] dog).

deponent-verbs.title: Unit 18: Deponent Verbs
deponent-verbs.chapter: 3. B1 The Great Divide: Verbal Aspect
deponent-verbs.content: Deponent Verbs (-ομαι) In Unit 9, we learned about the three main groups of Greek verbs. - Type A : End in an unaccented -ω (κάνω, παίζω) - Type B : End in an accented -ώ / -άω (αγαπώ, μπορώ) Both of these are Active verbs. The subject is actively doing something to an object. - Κάνω καφέ. (I make coffee). Now it is time to meet Type C : The Passive Verbs. 1. The Passive Voice vs. Deponent Verbs In Greek, the "Passive Voice" means that the subject is receiving the action, not doing it. Active: The dog bites the man. (The dog is doing it) Passive: The man is bitten by the dog. (The man is receiving it) When a Greek verb goes into the Passive Voice, its ending completely changes. It stops ending in -ω and instead ends in -ομαι (o-me) . But here is where things get strange. There is a massive group of Greek verbs that only exist in the Passive Voice format , but they have an Active meaning ! These are called Deponent Verbs . They look passive (they end in -ομαι ), but they act active. Here are some of the most common Deponent Verbs in the Greek language: - έρχομαι (I come / I arrive) - γίνομαι (I become / I happen) - χρειάζομαι (I need) - εργάζομαι (I work) - σκέφτομαι (I think) You cannot say έρχω or γίνω . Those words do not exist. You must use the -ομαι endings. 2. Conjugating Deponent Verbs (Present Tense) If you see a verb ending in -ομαι , you must use the passive endings. They look very different from the active endings, but they follow a strict pattern. Notice how almost every ending has an 'αι' (e) sound in it! Pronoun The Passive Ending Example (έρχομαι) English (Εγώ) -ομαι έρχ ομαι I come (Εσύ) -εσαι έρχ εσαι You come (Αυτό/ή/ό) -εται έρχ εται He/She/It comes (Εμείς) -όμαστε ερχ όμαστε * We come (Εσείς) -εστε έρχ εστε You come (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ονται έρχ ονται They come (Notice that the accent mark moves forward in the 'We' form: ερχόμαστε!) 3. Practice Sentences Look at how these verbs function exactly like normal active verbs in a sentence, even though they have passive endings. Από πού έρχεσαι; (Where are you coming from?) Ο Γιώργος χρειάζεται ένα αυτοκίνητο. (George needs a car.) Notice that the verb takes a Direct Object (το αυτοκίνητο) exactly like an active verb! Τι γίνεσαι; (What are you becoming? / How are you doing?) This is a very common alternative to "Τι κάνεις;" Εμείς εργαζόμαστε κάθε μέρα. (We work every day.) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that Deponent Verbs end in -ομαι instead of -ω. [ ] I know that even though they have "passive" endings, they have an active meaning (I come, I need). [ ] I have memorized the Passive Present Endings ( -ομαι, -εσαι, -εται, -όμαστε, -εστε, -ονται ).

dialogues-travel.title: dialogues-travel
dialogues-travel.content: Travel Dialogues When traveling in Greece, knowing how to ask for directions and handle common travel situations is essential. Asking Where Something Is Πού είναι η τουαλέτα; (Where is the bathroom?) Πού είναι η στάση; (Where is the bus stop?) Πού είναι η έξοδος; (Where is the exit?) Asking How to Get Somewhere Πώς πάω στην πλατεία; (How do I get to the square?) Πώς πάω στην Ακρόπολη; (How do I get to the Acropolis?) Direction Vocabulary Ευθεία (Straight) Αριστερά (Left) Δεξιά (Right) Εδώ / Εκεί (Here / There) Travel Tip In Greece, a nod of the head upward often means "No" (Όχι), while a nod downward means "Yes" (Ναι). This can be confusing for visitors at first!

diminutives.title: Unit 29: Diminutives and Augmentatives
diminutives.content: Diminutives & Augmentatives (Υποκοριστικά & Μεγεθυντικά) Greek is a language of intense emotion. You don't just drink a coffee; you drink a little coffee with a friend. You don't just see a dog; you see a huge, monstrous dog . To express these feelings of smallness, affection, or enormous size, Greek uses special suffixes attached to the end of nouns. 1. The Diminutives (Making things small or cute) Diminutives are incredibly common in spoken Greek. They are used to make things sound smaller, cuter, more polite, or more affectionate. When you order food, you rarely order a "τυρόπιτα" (cheese pie). You order a "τυροπιτάκι" (little cheese pie), because it sounds friendlier! Suffix 1: -άκι (The universal tiny-maker) This is by far the most common diminutive. It can be added to almost any noun, and it always turns the word into a Neuter noun (Το). Το σπίτι (the house) ➔ Το σπιτ άκι (the little house) Το παιδί (the child) ➔ Το παιδ άκι (the little child) Η τυρόπιτα (the cheese pie) ➔ Το τυροπιτ άκι (the little cheese pie) Ο καφές (the coffee) ➔ Το καφεδ άκι (the little coffee) (Notice how even masculine and feminine nouns become neuter when you add -άκι!) Suffix 2: -ίτσα / -ούλα (For Feminine Nouns) If you want to keep a word feminine while making it cute, you can use these suffixes. Η πόρτα (the door) ➔ Η πορτ ίτσα (the little door) Η αγάπη (the love) ➔ Η αγαπ ούλα μου (my sweet little love) Η Ελένη (Eleni) ➔ Η Ελεν ίτσα (Little Eleni) Suffix 3: -ούλης / -άκος (For Masculine Nouns) If you want to make a masculine person or animal sound cute or endearing. Ο παππούς (the grandfather) ➔ Ο παππ ούλης (the sweet old grandpa) Ο Γιώργος (George) ➔ Ο Γιωργ άκος (Little George) 2. The Augmentatives (Making things HUGE) Sometimes you need to emphasize that something is massive, impressive, or even a bit scary. For this, Greek uses Augmentative suffixes. These suffixes always turn the noun into a Masculine noun (Ο), or sometimes Feminine (Η) for certain words. Suffix 1: -αρος (The Giant) This is the most common masculine augmentative. Το σκυλί (the dog) ➔ Ο σκύλ αρος (the massive beast of a dog) Το αυτοκίνητο (the car) ➔ Ο αυτοκινητ άρας (the huge, impressive car) Ο άντρας (the man) ➔ Ο αντρ αρος (the big, burly man) Suffix 2: -κλα (The Huge Feminine) This suffix creates a sense of enormous, sometimes overwhelming size. Η πόρτα (the door) ➔ Η πορτά κλα (the gigantic door) Η φωνή (the voice) ➔ Η φωνά κλα (the huge, booming voice) 3. The Power of Emotion Remember, these suffixes aren't just about physical size. They are about emotion . If a friend invites you for a <καφεδάκι> (little coffee), they aren't talking about a small cup. They are talking about a relaxed, friendly, intimate chat. If someone calls their new car an <αυτοκινητάρας> , they are expressing pride and awe. Mastering these suffixes is the key to sounding like a true native speaker! 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that -άκι makes words small/cute, and always turns them into Το (Neuter). [ ] I know the feminine diminutives -ίτσα and -ούλα . [ ] I know the masculine diminutives -ούλης and -άκος . [ ] I know that -αρος and -κλα are used to make things sound completely massive!

double-letters.title: double-letters
double-letters.content: Double Letters (Digraphs) Now that you know the 24 letters of the Greek alphabet, we need to talk about digraphs . Simply put, sometimes Greek takes two letters and glues them together to create a brand new sound. This happens in English all the time. Think about the word s and h . Together, they form sh . The same principle applies in Greek, and there are two types: Vowel Combinations and Consonant Combinations . 1. Vowel Combinations (Diphthongs) Greek loves vowel combinations, but here is the good news: thanks to a linguistic process over thousands of years, most of them just make an [ee] sound or an [e] sound. Combination Pronunciation Example in English Greek Example Translation αι [e] as in p e t E gg αι τία (etía) cause ει [ee] as in m ee t S ee εί μαι (ímai) I am οι [ee] as in m ee t S ee οι κογένεια (ikoyénia) family υι [ee] as in m ee t S ee υι οθετώ (io-thetó) to adopt ου [oo] as in b oo t M oo n ου ρανός (ouranós) sky [!TIP] Why all the "ee" sounds? You now know six different ways to write the [ee] sound in Greek ( ι , η , υ , ει , οι , υι ). - ει is mostly used for Verbs (e.g., έχ ει = he has). - οι is mostly used for Plural Nouns (e.g., φίλ οι = friends). - ι / η are mostly used for Singular Nouns. 2. The Chameleons: αυ & ευ The combinations αυ and ευ are special. The υ (Upsilon) completely loses its vowel sound and turns into a consonant—either a V or an F , depending on the letter that comes immediately after it . The "F" Sound (Before Hard Consonants) If the next letter is "hard" (voiceless), like κ, π, τ, θ, σ, φ, χ , the combination sounds like af or ef . - αυ τοκίνητο ➔ ( af -tokínito) = car - Ευ ρώπη ➔ ( Ef -rópi) = Europe The "V" Sound (Before Soft Consonants or Vowels) If the next letter is a vowel or "soft" (voiced), like γ, β, δ, ζ, λ, μ, ν, ρ , it sounds like av or ev . - αύ ριο ➔ ( áv -rio) = tomorrow - Ευ αγγέλιο ➔ ( Ev -angélio) = Gospel 3. Consonant Combinations Ancient Greek didn't have the sounds for B , D , or G as we know them in English today. To make these sounds in Modern Greek, you have to smash two consonants together. Combination Sound Greek Example Pronunciation Translation μπ (m+p) B as in B oy μπ α μπ άς b a b ás dad ντ (n+t) D as in D og ντ ύνω d íno to dress γκ / γγ (g+k) G as in G o ά γκ υρα á g ira anchor τσ (t+s) TS as in i ts τσ έπη ts épi pocket τζ (t+z) J / DZ as in j eep τζ άκι dz áki fireplace [!CAUTION] Wait, I thought Beta (Β) was 'B' and Delta (Δ) was 'D'? No! Remember from the previous lesson: Β sounds like V (Video), and Δ sounds like TH (There). So if you want to write "Bank" in Greek, you can't use Β . You have to use μπ ➔ Τράπεζα (Trápeza) or the English loan word Μπανκ . Practice: Putting it all together! Look at the word construction below and see how the digraphs shape the sound: ντ (D) + ου (oo) + λ (l) + ά (a) + π (p) + α (a) ➔ ντουλάπα (dou-lá-pa) = closet μπ (B) + ί (i) + ρ (r) + α (a) ➔ μπίρα (bí-ra) = beer

feminine-declension.title: feminine-declension
feminine-declension.content: Feminine Declension (Κλίση Θηλυκών) Feminine nouns are a breath of fresh air after the rules of the masculine table. They are highly consistent and very easy to memorize. Almost all feminine nouns end in -η or -α . Let's see how they change from being the Subject (Nominative) to being the Object (Accusative). 1. Nouns ending in -η Example: η γυναίκα (the woman) - wait, that ends in α! Example: η κόρη (the daughter) Case Singular English Nom (Subject) η κόρ η The daughter is here. Acc (Object) τη(ν) κόρ η I see the daughter . Gen (Owner) της κόρ ης The book of the daughter . What happens? If the word is the Object (Accusative), the noun itself does not change at all! Only the article changes from η to την . Note: Just like the masculine 'τον', the feminine 'την' only keeps its 'N' if the next word starts with a vowel or a hard consonant. Don't stress about this right now! 2. Nouns ending in -α Example: η γυναίκα (the woman) Case Singular English Nom (Subject) η γυναίκ α The woman is here. Acc (Object) τη(ν) γυναίκ α I see the woman . Gen (Owner) της γυναίκ ας The car of the woman . What happens? Again, absolutely nothing happens to the noun! Only the article changes to την . [!TIP] The Golden Rule for Feminine Singular In the singular, the noun never changes between being the subject (Nom) or the object (Acc). Only the article does! The Plural Forms When you want to talk about multiple daughters or women, the endings change to show Plurality. Here is the best part: in the plural, feminine nouns behave exactly the same whether they are the subject or the object! Ending Plural Nominative (Subjects) Plural Accusative (Objects) -η οι κόρ ες (the daughters) τις κόρ ες (the daughters) -α οι γυναίκ ες (the women) τις γυναίκ ες (the women) Look closely: - Οι γυναίκες είναι εδώ. (The women are here - Nominative). - Βλέπω τις γυναίκες . (I see the women - Accusative). The word γυναίκες didn't change at all! Only the article changed ( οι ➔ τις ). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that feminine singular nouns don't change their endings when they become the Object (Accusative). Only η becomes την . [ ] I know that the plural ending for almost all feminine nouns is -ες . [ ] I know that the plural article for subjects is οι , and for objects is τις .

future-continuous.title: Unit 11: The Continuous Future
future-continuous.content: The Continuous Future (Ο Εξακολουθητικός Μέλλοντας) You know how to talk about the Present (what you are doing right now). You know how to use the Subjunctive (what you want to do). Now, it is time to look forward. It is time to learn the Future Tense . In English, we create the future tense by adding the helper word "will" (I will play). Greek does exactly the same thing! 1. The Magic Word: Θα In Greek, the word for "will" is θα (tha) . Just like να and δεν , this is a tiny, powerful particle that sits directly in front of the verb. Also like να and δεν , it doesn't stand alone. It relies on the ending of the verb to tell us who is doing the action. English: I will play. Greek (Literal): Will I-play. Greek: Θα παίξω. (Wait, that's the Simple Future! Let's stick to what we know for now). 2. The Continuous Future Here is where Greek gets very specific (and very brilliant). In English, "I will play" can mean two entirely different things: 1. I will play a quick game of chess tomorrow. (A single, completed event) 2. I will be playing chess every night for the rest of my life. (A continuous, ongoing habit) Greek hates ambiguity. Greek forces you to choose between these two meanings by using different verb stems. This is called Verbal Aspect . For the entirety of Level A (A1 and A2), we are only going to focus on the Continuous Aspect . This means actions that are ongoing, repeated, or habitual. How do you form the Continuous Future? It is incredibly easy: You take the Present Tense verb you already know, and you just put "Θα" in front of it! English Greek Meaning I will play (repeatedly) Θα παίζω I will be playing (every day, continuously). You will run (always) Θα τρέχεις You will be running (as a habit). We will have Θα έχουμε We will continue to have. He will be Θα είναι He will be. Κάθε μέρα θα διαβάζω ένα βιβλίο. (Every day, I will [be reading] a book.) Because it happens "every day", it is a repeated habit. We use the Present stem (διαβάζ-). 3. The Power Combo: Δεν + Θα What happens when you want to say "I will not be playing"? You have two powerful particles: δεν (not) and θα (will). Which one wins the race to be closest to the verb? Rule: The future word (θα) is always glued closest to the verb. English: I will not run. Greek: Δεν θα τρέχω. (Or usually pronounced: Δε θα τρέχω ). And if there is a pronoun? The pronoun sneaks in right before the verb! - Δεν θα σε βλέπω. (I will not be seeing you). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the word θα means "will". [ ] I understand the difference between a one-time event (Simple) and an ongoing habit (Continuous). [ ] I know that Θα + Present Tense Verb = The Continuous Future (I will be doing X). [ ] I know the word order is Δεν + Θα + (Pronoun) + Verb .

genders.title: genders
genders.content: The Genders (Τα Γένη) - Exceptions and Rules In the last lesson, you learned that Ο , Η , and Το dictate whether a word is masculine, feminine, or neuter. You also learned that the ending of the word usually gives away the gender. But like any language, Greek has a few exceptions. To achieve fluency, you need to know how to spot the "spies"—words that look like one gender but are actually another. 1. The "Feminine Spies" ending in -ος The most common masculine ending is -ος (like ο φίλος = the friend). However, there is a small class of very common, very important words that end in -ος but are completely Feminine (Η) . These are almost always geographical terms or words stolen from Ancient Greek. η οδός (ee o-dós) the street (formal name) η λεωφόρος (ee le-o-fó-ros) the avenue η οθόνη Wait, wait. It's η μέθοδος (the method), η έξοδος (the exit). Let's stick to the common ones! The Three Most Common Feminine "-ος" Words: 1. η οδός (the street - used for addresses) 2. η λεωφόρος (the avenue) 3. η ψήφος (the vote) 4. Certain islands: η Κύπρος (Cyprus), η Ρόδος (Rhodes) [!CAUTION] If a Greek asks you where you live, you would say "Στην οδό Ερμού" (On Ermou street ). Even though it ends in -ο , it behaves according to feminine grammatical rules! 2. Foreign Loanwords What happens when Greek adopts a word from English or French? For example, "computer", "internet", or "bar"? Greek doesn't know what gender these words are supposed to be. So, it simply dumps almost all foreign loanwords into the Neuter (Το) category. Furthermore, foreign loanwords never change their shape . They are invariable. το ίντερνετ (to in-ter-net) = the internet το μπαρ (to bar) = the bar το σινεμά (to si-ne-má) = the cinema το ταξί (to ta-xí) = the taxi Even though το ίντερνετ ends in a consonant (which native Greek neuter words never do), it is neuter purely because it's a foreign word. 3. The Neuter "-ος" Words We just talked about feminine words ending in -ος . Well, there's another curveball. Some neuter words also end in -ος ! These describe abstract concepts or states of being. The Three Most Common Neuter "-ος" Words: 1. το λάθος (the mistake) 2. το βάθος (the depth / background) 3. το μέγεθος (the size) 4. το κράτος (the state / nation) These are very advanced for your current level, but you will hear Greeks say «Κάνεις λάθος» (You are making a mistake). Notice it receives the neuter article το , not the masculine ο . 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist Don't let these exceptions scare you. 95% of the time, the rules from the last lesson apply flawlessly. Just remember: - [ ] If a word is borrowed from English (like taxi or internet ), it is always Το . - [ ] Words like η οδός (street) are feminine despite ending in -ος . - [ ] Words like το λάθος (mistake) are neuter despite ending in -ος .

genitive-absolute.title: Unit 34: Participles in the Genitive Absolute
genitive-absolute.content: Participles in the Genitive Absolute (Γενική Απόλυτη) In Level B2 (Unit 24), you learned about Participles. - The Active Participle (-οντας): "While running..." - The Passive Participle (-μένος): "The broken glass..." But there is a "secret" third type of participle structure. It is a ghost from Ancient Greek that survived in modern, highly formal Katharevousa-style texts. It is called the Genitive Absolute (Η Γενική Απόλυτη Μετοχή). You will rarely hear this spoken in a coffee shop, but you will absolutely see it in newspapers, official documents, literature, and news broadcasts. 1. What is the Genitive Absolute? Sometimes you want to connect two actions that happen at the same time, but they have two different subjects . In normal everyday Greek, you just use the word ενώ (while) or επειδή (because): - Επειδή έβρεχε , μείναμε μέσα. (Because it was raining, we stayed inside). - Ενώ μιλούσε ο Γιώργος , χτύπησε το τηλέφωνο. (While George was speaking, the phone rang). To sound incredibly formal and elevated, C2 speakers rip out the connector words (while/because), erase the verb, and replace the whole clause with Nouns and Participles in the Genitive Case . 2. The Formula Instead of a normal sentence with a Nominative subject and a normal verb, you create an "Absolute" bubble out of Genitive words. [Noun in Genitive] + [Participle in Genitive] Let's look at the Passive Participle ending in Genitive: -μένου / -μένης / -μένων . Example 1: Given That... Normal Greek: Επειδή αυτά τα πράγματα είναι δεδομένα... (Since these things are given...) Genitive Absolute: Δεδομένων αυτών των πραγμάτων... (These things being given...) Example 2: In the event of... Normal Greek: Αν γίνει σεισμός... (If an earthquake happens...) Genitive Absolute: Γινομένου σεισμού... (An earthquake happening...) Example 3: God willing... Normal Greek: Αν θέλει ο Θεός... (If God wants...) Genitive Absolute: Θεού θέλοντος... (God willing...) 3. The Most Common Frozen Expressions While you can technically build a Genitive Absolute phrasing from scratch, in Modern Greek, people almost exclusively rely on a handful of highly recognizable, frozen expressions. As a C2 learner, you simply need to memorize these phrases like vocabulary words! The Genitive Absolute Literal Translation True Meaning Θεού θέλοντος God willing Hopefully / God willing Καλώς εχόντων των πραγμάτων Things having well All being well / Hopefully Μη γένοιτο! Let it not happen God forbid! (Actually a rare optative form, usually grouped here). Τουναντίον... Of the opposite On the contrary... Απουσιάζοντος του καθηγητού The teacher being absent With the teacher absent Τρέχοντος του έτους The year running During the current year Καλώς εχόντων των πραγμάτων, θα τελειώσουμε το έργο αύριο. (All going well, we will finish the project tomorrow). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the Genitive Absolute is an ancient, highly formal way of connecting two clauses. [ ] I understand it is built using a Genitive Subject + a Genitive Participle. [ ] I have memorized the most common C2 expressions: Θεού θέλοντος (God willing) and Καλώς εχόντων των πραγμάτων (All being well).

genitive-case.title: Unit 13: Possessive Pronouns & Genitive
genitive-case.content: The Genitive Case (Γενική Πτώση) You already know the Nominative Case (Subjects) and the Accusative Case (Objects). It is time to learn the third major case: the Genitive Case . The Genitive case has one primary job in Greek: Possession . It is the Greek equivalent of adding an apostrophe "s" ('s) or saying "of the" in English. 1. How to form the Genitive When a person or thing becomes an "Owner", both the article and the ending of the noun must change. A. The Genitive Articles Let's review the articles for the Genitive case. They actually have a very strong pattern! Gender Singular Owner Plural Owners Masculine του (of the man) των (of the men) Feminine της (of the woman) των (of the women) Neuter του (of the child) των (of the children) [!TIP] Notice that του is used for both masculine and neuter singular owners. Notice that των is used for ALL plural owners, regardless of gender! If you see the word των , you know instantly that something is owned by multiple people. B. The Genitive Noun Endings How do the actual nouns change when they become owners? Let's look at the singular forms: Masculine (-ος, -ας, -ης): The noun drops its ending and becomes -ου (for the -ος group) or drops its -ς (for the other groups). - Ο φίλος ➔ του φίλ ου (of the friend) - Ο πατέρας ➔ του πατέρ α (of the father) Feminine (-η, -α): The noun ALWAYS adds an -ς to the end! - Η μητέρα ➔ της μητέρ ας (of the mother) - Η κόρη ➔ της κόρ ης (of the daughter) Neuter (-ο, -ι, -μα): The noun becomes -ου or adds a syllable. - Το βιβλίο ➔ του βιβλί ου (of the book) - Το παιδί ➔ του παιδ ιού (of the child) - Το μάθημα ➔ του μαθήματ ος (of the lesson) (Don't stress too much about the plurals right now, but just as a sneak peek: almost all plural owners end in -ων ! Example: των φίλων, των κοριτσιών). 2. Possession in Practice In English, we usually put the owner first: "The teacher's book". In Greek, you must use the "of the" structure: "The book of the teacher ". The thing being owned comes first (usually in the Nominative or Accusative), followed by the Owner (in the Genitive). Let's look at some examples: English: The boy's dog. (Or: The dog of the boy ). Greek: Ο σκύλος του αγοριού . (Ο σκύλος is the subject, το αγόρι changed to του αγοριού to show ownership). English: I see Maria's car. (Or: I see the car of Maria ). Greek: Βλέπω το αυτοκίνητο της Μαρίας . (Notice how Maria gained an -ς? Η Μαρία ➔ της Μαρίας). English: The color of the house. Greek: Το χρώμα του σπιτιού . 3. Relationships vs Possession The Genitive isn't just for owning physical objects; it's also used to express relationships between people, concepts, or parts of a whole. Family: Η αδερφή του Γιώργου (George's sister) Parts: Η πόρτα του δωματίου (The door of the room) Names: Το όνομα της δασκάλας (The teacher's name) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the Genitive case is used to show ownership or relationships (the "of the" or "'s" rule). [ ] I know the singular articles for owners ( του, της, του ) and the universal plural article ( των ). [ ] I know that Greek always structures possession as "The X of the Y" (Το αυτοκίνητο της Μαρίας).

have-verb.title: have-verb
have-verb.content: The Verb 'To Have' (Έχω) Now that you know how to say "I am" ( είμαι ), it's time to learn the second most important verb in Greek: "I have" ( έχω ). Together, these two verbs will unlock hundreds of sentences! The dictionary form of this verb is έχω (é-ho) . 1. Conjugating "Έχω" (I have) Remember the "Pro-Drop" rule from the last lesson? You don't need to say "Εγώ" (I) or "Εσύ" (You) before the verb. The ending of the verb tells us exactly who is doing the having. Look closely at how the end of the word changes: Pronoun Verb English Pronunciation (Εγώ) έχω I have (é-ho) (Εσύ) έχεις You have (é-his) (Αυτό/ή/ό) έχει He/She/It has (é-hi) (Εμείς) έχουμε We have (é-hou-me) (Εσείς) έχετε You have (é-he-te) (Αυτοί/ές/ά) έχουν(ε) They have (é-houn) [!TIP] The Secret "O" Ending Almost every verb in the Greek dictionary ends in -ω (the letter Omega). Just like έχω ! If you see a verb ending in -ω , you instantly know it means "I [do something]". - παίζ ω (I play) - τρέχ ω (I run) - θέλ ω (I want) 2. Using "Έχω" in Sentences Just like in English, we use "έχω" to talk about possession, family, and physical objects. Here is where your knowledge from Unit 2 (The Accusative Case) kicks in! When you have something, that "something" is the Object of the sentence. It is receiving the action of being had. Therefore, whatever you have must be in the Accusative Case . Look at the examples below. Notice how the articles are τον , την , or ένα : Έχω ένα σκύλο. I have a dog. Έχεις το βιβλίο; Do you have the book? Ο Γιώργος έχει μία αδερφή. George has a sister. Έχουμε τον χρόνο. We have the time. 3. The Power Combo: Έχω + Είμαι With just these two verbs and the nouns you've learned so far, you can build full, natural conversations! Person A: Είσαι εδώ; (Are you here?) Person B: Ναι, είμαι εδώ. (Yes, I am here.) Person A: Έχεις τον χάρτη; (Do you have the map?) Person B: Όχι, ο Γιάννης έχει τον χάρτη. (No, Giannis has the map.) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know the conjugation of έχω (έχω, έχεις, έχει, έχουμε, έχετε, έχουν). [ ] I know that the object I am holding or possessing must be in the Accusative case (τον, την, το). [ ] I know that verbs ending in -ω mean "I do".

idioms-proverbs.title: idioms-proverbs
idioms-proverbs.content: Idiomatic Expressions & Proverbs Content for Idiomatic Expressions & Proverbs goes here.

idioms.title: Unit 36: Idiomatic Expressions & Proverbs
idioms.content: Idiomatic Expressions & Proverbs Congratulations. You have reached the final lesson of the C2 Greek Curriculum. You know the grammar, you know the vocabulary, and you can understand complex, advanced sentences. But true fluency isn't just about grammar. It's about culture. Greek relies heavily on idioms, metaphors, and ancient proverbs that make absolutely no sense when translated literally. Here are the most iconic Greek idioms you must know. 1. Expressions of Dismissal & Frustration When Greeks are annoyed, they don't hold back. Τρέχα γύρευε (Run and seek) Meaning: "It's a lost cause" or "Good luck with that." Used when a situation is hopelessly complicated or something is impossible to find. - Πού είναι ο διευθυντής; (Where is the manager?) - Τρέχα γύρευε... (Good luck finding out...) Κάνω την πάπια (I play the duck) Meaning: "I play dumb" or "I pretend not to know." Used when someone is ignoring an issue or pretending they have no idea what is going on. - Του είπα να πληρώσει και αυτός κάνει την πάπια ! (I told him to pay and he is playing dumb!) Τον έγραψα στα παλιά μου τα παπούτσια (I wrote him on my old shoes) Meaning: "I completely ignored him" / "I don't give a damn about him." A very strong (but not offensive) way to say you completely disregarded someone's opinion or presence. 2. Expressions of Trouble / Disaster Τα έκανα θάλασσα (I made a sea out of it) Meaning: "I messed everything up" / "I made a huge mistake." Used when you completely botch a task or a test. - Πώς πήγαν οι εξετάσεις; (How did the exams go?) - Τα έκανα θάλασσα... (I messed it all up...) Έγινε της κακομοίρας (It became of the ill-fated woman) Meaning: "All hell broke loose" / "It was absolute chaos." Used to describe a crazy fight, a massive argument, or a chaotic situation. - Όταν έμαθε την αλήθεια, έγινε της κακομοίρας . (When he learned the truth, all hell broke loose). 3. Classic Greek Proverbs Proverbs (Παροιμίες) are pieces of old wisdom passed down through generations. Greeks use them constantly in daily speech to prove a point. Όποιος βιάζεται σκοντάφτει. (Whoever hurries, trips). Meaning: Haste makes waste. Take your time and do things properly. Αλλού τα κακαρίσματα κι αλλού γεννούν οι κότες. (The clucking is over here, but the hens are laying eggs over there). Meaning: The real action is happening somewhere completely different from where the noise is. (Used when someone is distracted by a fake issue, or a politician is making a decoy statement). Έλειπε ο Μάρτης από την Σαρακοστή; (Could March be missing from Lent?) Meaning: Used when someone always, inevitably shows up to an event (usually uninvited or predictably). "There he is again... of course he's here." 4. The Ultimate Greek Word: Φιλότιμο If there is one concept you take away from Greek culture, let it be Φιλότιμο (Filótimo). Literally, it means "Friend of honor". There is no direct translation in English. It describes a deep, unconditional sense of personal pride, duty, courage, respect, and doing the right thing simply because it is the right thing to do , even if it requires personal sacrifice. Δεν το έκανε για τα λεφτά, το έκανε από φιλότιμο . (He didn't do it for the money, he did it out of filotimo ). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that "playing the duck" (κάνω την πάπια) means to play dumb. [ ] I know that "making it a sea" (τα έκανα θάλασσα) means I messed up. [ ] I understand the concept of Φιλότιμο (Honor/Pride/Duty). 🎉 ΣΥΓΧΑΡΗΤΗΡΙΑ! CONGRATULATIONS! 🎉 You have officially completed the C2 Masterclass in Modern Greek. You now possess the grammatical tools to understand native speakers, read Greek literature, and navigate the beautiful, complex landscape of the Greek language. Καλό ταξίδι! (Have a good journey!)

imperative.title: Unit 19: The Imperative (Commands)
imperative.chapter: 3. B1 The Great Divide: Verbal Aspect
imperative.content: The Imperative (Η Προστακτική) The Imperative Mood is used to give commands, orders, or strong advice. In English, we just yell the base verb at someone: "Run!", "Close the door!", "Stop!" In Greek, giving a command is deeply tied to Aspect (Continuous vs. Simple). Are you commanding someone to do something right now, once ? Or are you commanding someone to do something repeatedly as a habit ? Let's look at the Simple (One-time) Commands first, because they are the most common. 1. The Simple Command (Do it now!) If you want someone to do a single action and finish it (like "Shut the door" or "Write your name"), you must build a command using the Simple Stem (The Aorist Stem). There are two people you can give commands to: 1. Εσύ (You singular - Informal) 2. Εσείς (You plural / You formal) Giving Informal Commands (Εσύ) To tell one person to do something right now: Take the Simple Stem, and add an -ε or an -ε sound. - γράφω ➔ γράψ- ➔ Γράψε! (Write!) - διαβάζω ➔ διαβάσ- ➔ Διάβασε! (Read!) - κλείνω ➔ κλείσ- ➔ Κλείσε! (Close!) Giving Formal/Plural Commands (Εσείς) To tell multiple people (or someone you respect) to do something right now: Take the Simple Stem, and add -τε (or -στε for verbs that changed to 's'). - Γράψτε! (Write, guys!) - Διαβάστε! (Read, class!) - Κλείστε! (Close it, sir!) 2. The Continuous Command (Keep doing it!) What if you want to say "Keep running!", or "Always read before bed"? You are asking for a continuous, ongoing habit or action. Therefore, you must use the Continuous Stem (The Present Stem). Giving Informal Commands (Εσύ) Take the Present Stem, and literally just use the "You" ending without the final -s! - Τρέχε! (Keep running!) - Διάβαζε! (Read [every day]!) Giving Formal/Plural Commands (Εσείς) This is the easiest one of all. Exploring the present tense, the Formal Command is identical to the normal Present Tense "You guys" form! - Τρέχετε! (Keep running, guys!) - Διαβάζετε! (Keep reading, class!) 3. The Negative Command (Don't do it!) Here is the best news you will hear all day. Greek does not have special negative command words. If you want to say "Don't do that!", you just use the Subjunctive Mood (Unit 17) with the negative word μη(ν) ! That's it! "Don't run" is literally just "Not you-run". Μην κλείσεις την πόρτα! (Don't close the door [this once]!) Μην τρέχεις! (Don't run [in general]!) (Remember: We use the word Μην instead of Δεν whenever we are giving commands or expressing wishes!) 4. The 5 Crazy Irregulars Because commands are used all the time in daily life, many of the most common verbs have totally irregular shapes when yelled as a command. You must memorize these 5 everyday commands. They do not follow the rules! Λέω (I say) ➔ Πες! (Say it!) / Πείτε! (Say it, guys!) Βλέπω (I see) ➔ Δες! (Look!) / Δείτε! (Look, guys!) Βρίσκω (I find) ➔ Βρες! (Find it!) / Βρείτε! (Find it, guys!) Πίνω (I drink) ➔ Πιες! (Drink!) / Πιείτε! (Drink, guys!) Έρχομαι (I come) ➔ Έλα! (Come here!) / Ελάτε! (Come here, guys!) (Pro-Tip: "Έλα!" is probably the most commonly used word in Greece. It means "Come here", "Come on", "Hello [on the phone]", and "Get out of here / No way!") 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know how to make a Simple Command using the Simple Stem + ε / τε ( Γράψε / Γράψτε ). [ ] I know how to make a Negative Command using Μην + the Subjunctive verb ( Μην γράψεις ). [ ] I have memorized the 5 crazy irregular commands ( Πες, Δες, Βρες, Πιες, Έλα ).

imperfect-tense.title: Unit 12: The Imperfect Tense
imperfect-tense.content: The Imperfect Tense (Ο Παρατατικός) In the last lesson, we learned about Verbal Aspect : the difference between a one-time completed event, and a continuous, repeated habit. When talking about the Past in Greek, Aspect is everything. In this lesson, we will explore the Continuous Past (also known as the Imperfect Tense). This is the tense you use to talk about: 1. Things you used to do regularly. ("I used to play tennis.") 2. Things you were doing when something else happened. ("I was sleeping when the phone rang.") Because this tense is continuous, we use the Present Tense stem (the same stem we use for "habitual" actions). 1. The Rule of the Augment To make a verb Past Tense, Greek does something very strange: it glues an extra letter onto the front of the word. This extra letter is called the Augment , and it is almost always the letter ε (Epsilon). Why does Greek do this? The rule is rhythm. Greek demands that the stress mark (the accent) must fall on the third-to-last syllable in any past tense verb. If the verb is too short (only two syllables long), it doesn't have a third-to-last syllable! So we have to artificially add one to the front. Present: παίζω (I play) - 2 syllables (παί - ζω) Past Step 1: Add the 'ε' ➔ επαίζω (ε - παί - ζω) Past Step 2: Move the accent ➔ έ παιζω 2. The Past Endings We have our augmented stem ( έπαιζ- ). Now we just need the past tense endings! The endings for the Imperfect Tense (Continuous Past) are identical for everyone, whether it's an action you did once (Simple) or repeatedly (Continuous). Pronoun Past Ending Example (έπαιζα) (Εγώ) -α έπαιζ α (Εσύ) -ες έπαιζ ες (Αυτό/ή/ό) -ε έπαιζ ε (Εμείς) -αμε παίζ αμε * (Εσείς) -ατε παίζ ατε * (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -αν(ε) έπαιζ αν(ε) *Wait, what happened to the 'ε' in We/You?! Remember the rule: The accent must fall on the third-to-last syllable. Look at (Εμείς) παίζαμε. Let's count the syllables starting from the end: με (1), ζα (2), παί (3). The word is already three syllables long! It doesn't need the extra 'ε' at the front anymore! The augment naturally falls off. 3. Practice Sentences Let's look at how the Continuous Past is used in real life. Notice how all of these situations describe something that was ongoing or a repeated habit . Κάθε μέρα έτρεχα στο πάρκο. (Every day, I used to run in the park.) Because it happened "every day", it was a habit. We use the Continuous Past. Ο Γιώργος διάβαζε ενώ η Μαρία έπαιζε. (George was reading while Maria was playing.) Both actions were ongoing in the background. Continuous Past! Δεν ξέραμε. (We didn't know.) Knowing something is an ongoing state, not a one-time snap. We drop the 'ε' augment because the word is long enough: (ξέ-ρα-με). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the Imperfect Tense is used for habits ( used to do ) or ongoing background actions ( was doing ). [ ] I know that short verbs gain an έ- augment at the beginning. [ ] I know that the accent always falls on the 3rd-to-last syllable for past tense verbs. [ ] I have memorized the past endings ( -α, -ες, -ε, -αμε, -ατε, -αν ).

impersonal-verbs.title: Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions
impersonal-verbs.content: Impersonal Verbs & Expressions (Απρόσωπα Ρήματα) Every sentence needs a subject, right? "I eat", "The dog barks", "The man runs". But what happens when there isn't a clear subject? - "It is raining." (What is raining? The sky?) - "It is forbidden." (What is forbidden? The universe?) - "It seems that..." (What seems?) In Greek, when there is no specific person doing the action, we use a special group of verbs called Impersonal Verbs . Because "It" is the imaginary subject, these verbs only ever exist in the 3rd Person Singular (the Αυτό form). 1. The Two Titans: Πρέπει and Μπορεί You have already been using the two most common impersonal verbs since Level A1! Πρέπει (να) : "It is necessary that..." ➔ Must / Have to Μπορεί (να) : "It is possible that..." ➔ May / Might The Subjunctive Trigger Because impersonal verbs don't have a real subject, they almost always trigger the Subjunctive Mood (the word να ). The verb that comes after να tells us who the real subject of the sentence is! English: I must go. Greek (Literal): It is necessary that I go. Greek: Πρέπει να πάω. English: He might sleep. Greek (Literal): It is possible that he sleeps. Greek: Μπορεί να κοιμηθεί. [!WARNING] Μπορώ vs Μπορεί The verb "to be able to" exists as a normal, personal verb ( μπορώ - I can). But when it is frozen in the 3rd person ( μπορεί ), it loses its personal owner and translates to "it is possible" / "might". - Μπορώ να πάω. (I can go - I have the ability). - Μπορεί να πάω. (I might go - It is a possibility). 2. Other Common Impersonal Verbs Here is a list of extremely common impersonal verbs that you will hear daily. Remember: they only ever exist in this frozen -ει form, and they are usually followed by να ! Greek Verb Literal Meaning Real Meaning Απαγορεύεται (να) It is forbidden You are not allowed to... Επιτρέπεται (να) It is permitted You are allowed to... Φαίνεται (ότι) It seems It looks like... Αξίζει (να) It is worth It's worth... Συμβαίνει (να) It happens It happens to... Πρόκειται (για / να) It is about It is about / It is going to... Απαγορεύεται να καπνίζεις εδώ. (It is forbidden to smoke here / You are not allowed to smoke here). Αξίζει να διαβάσεις αυτό το βιβλίο. (It is worth reading this book / This book is worth reading). Πρόκειται να βρέξει. (It is going to rain). 3. Impersonal Expressions (Είναι + Adjective) You don't always need a special verb to create an impersonal sentence. You can just use the frozen word Είναι (It is) followed by an adjective! This also directly triggers the να subjunctive structure. Είναι δύσκολο να... (It is difficult to...) Είναι εύκολο να... (It is easy to...) Είναι λογικό να... (It makes sense to...) Είναι κρίμα να... (It's a pity to...) Είναι δύσκολο να μάθεις ελληνικά, αλλά αξίζει! (It is difficult to learn Greek, but it's worth it!) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that Impersonal Verbs are frozen in the 3rd Person Singular (usually ending in -ει or -εται ). [ ] I know that they usually trigger the Subjunctive word να . [ ] I know the difference between μπορώ να (I can) and μπορεί να (I might). [ ] I have memorized the most common impersonal verbs ( πρέπει, απαγορεύεται, φαίνεται, αξίζει ).

indefinite-pronouns.title: indefinite-pronouns
indefinite-pronouns.content: Indefinite Pronouns Indefinite pronouns refer to people or things in a non-specific way. Greek has a rich set of these pronouns, some of which are declinable and others which are stable (indeclinable). κάποιος, -α, -ο Somebody / Someone Refering to an unknown specific person. κάτι Something Indeclinable. Used for unspecified things. κάθε / καθένας Each / Every κάθε is indeclinable; καθένας declines like ένας . κανένας / καμία / κανένα Nobody / No one Also means "anyone" in questions. τίποτα Nothing / Anything Indeclinable. Used in negative sentences. άλλος, -η, -ο Another / Other Declines like a standard adjective. Usage in Sentences Κάποιος είναι εκεί. ( Someone is there.) Δεν θέλω τίποτα . (I don't want anything/nothing .) Κάθε μέρα. ( Every day.)

interjections.title: interjections
interjections.content: The Interjections Interjections (Επιφωνήματα) are words that express sudden emotions or reactions. They are often used in spoken Greek to add color and feeling to a conversation. Μπράβο!Bravo! / Well done! Α μπά!No way! / Don't tell me! Άντε! / Άιντε!Come on! / Let's go! Αχ!Oh! / Ah! (pain or sigh) Μακάρι!I wish! / Hopefully! Αλίμονο!Alas! / Unfortunately! Εύγε!Excellent! / Bravo! Cultural Highlight: "Άντε" Άντε is one of the most common words in Greece. Depending on the tone, it can mean "Come on", "Let's go", "Hurry up", or even "Get out of here!" (in disbelief).

interrogative-pronouns.title: Unit 7: Asking Questions
interrogative-pronouns.content: Asking Questions (Οι Ερωτήσεις) You now have a solid vocabulary and an understanding of verb conjugation. The last skill you need to survive in Greece is the ability to ask for things! In English, we call these the "W" words (Who, What, Where, When, Why). In Greek, they are called Interrogative Pronouns . Let's look at the "Big Five": 1. The Universal "What" (Τι) The word for "what" is τι (ti) . It is the easiest word in the Greek language because it never changes . It doesn't care about gender, number, or case. Τι είναι αυτό; (What is this?) Τι κάνεις; (What are you doing? / How are you?) Τι ώρα είναι; (What time is it?) 2. The Chameleon "Who" (Ποιος) The word for "who" is tricky. Because Greek grammar Demands to know the gender of everything, you can't just say "who". You have to specify "Who (male)", "Who (female)", or "Which (thing)" . This means Ποιος acts exactly like an adjective! It changes its ending based on the noun it replaces. Gender Word Pronunciation Example Masc Ποιος (pyós) Ποιος είναι ο άντρας; (Who is the man?) Fem Ποια (pyá) Ποια είναι η γυναίκα; (Who is the woman?) Neut Ποιο (pyó) Ποιο παιδί; (Which child?) [!CAUTION] Who vs. Which Notice from the examples above that Ποιος / Ποια / Ποιο translates to both "Who" and "Which". - If you use it alone, it means "Who?" ( Ποιος είναι;) - If you put it in front of a noun, it means "Which?" ( Ποιο βιβλίο; = Which book?) 3. The Chameleon "How Much/Many" (Πόσος) Just like "ποιος", the word for "how much" or "how many" acts like an adjective and changes its ending to match the noun it is asking about. Gender Singular (How Much) Plural (How Many) Masc Πόσ ος Πόσ οι Fem Πόσ η Πόσ ες Neut Πόσ ο Πόσ α Πόσο κάνει; (How much does it cost?) Πόσα παιδιά έχεις; (How many children do you have? [Notice -α to match -α] ) 4. Where and When (Πού & Πότε) These two are very easy. They never change their shape. Πού (poo) = Where? Πότε (pó-te) = When? Πού είναι το ξενοδοχείο; (Where is the hotel?) Πότε φεύγουμε; (When are we leaving?) [!TIP] The Secret Accent Marks Remember back in Unit 1 when we said that one-syllable words don't get accent marks? Πού (Where) and Πώς (How) are the exceptions! They keep their accent marks so you don't confuse them with the identical words meaning "that". If you see an accent on a tiny word, it's a question! 🎯 A1 Foundation Checklist Congratulations! You have officially completed the A1 Foundations Stage. You can now: - [ ] Read the Greek Alphabet and pronounce double letters. - [ ] Understand the 3 Genders and the concept of Declension (Cases). - [ ] Conjugate Type A verbs in the Present Tense ( -ω, -εις, -ει... ). - [ ] Make sentences negative ( δεν ) and ask questions ( τι, πού, ποιος... ). In the next phase (A2 Stage), we will learn how to talk about the Future and the Past, starting with the mysterious "Continuous Aspect".

irregular-verbs-nouns.title: irregular-verbs-nouns
