irregular-words.title: Unit 35: Irregular & Defective Words
irregular-words.content: Irregular & Defective Words You have mastered the rules of Greek grammar. You know how to decline nouns, conjugate verbs, and build complex sentences. But every language has its rebels. In Greek, there are words that flat-out refuse to follow the rules, and words that are "defective" (missing pieces!). At Level C2, you need to know about these eccentrics. 1. Nouns with Multiple Personalities (Double Plurals) Some nouns in Greek are completely schizophrenic. They change gender when they become plural, or they offer you two entirely different plural forms depending on what you mean! The Gender-Benders There are several highly common masculine nouns (ending in -ος) that suddenly decide to become Neuter (ending in -α) when they turn plural. Ο χρόνος (the year) ➔ Τα χρόνια (the years). (Never say "οι χρόνοι" unless you are talking about grammar tenses!) Ο λόγος (the reason/word) ➔ Τα λόγια (the words). (Though "οι λόγοι" is used for "the reasons"). The "A-La-Carte" Plurals The word for "Fox" (η αλεπού) is an old borrowing. Because it ends in a weird vowel, it couldn't decide how to be plural. So it has two accepted plurals: - Η αλεπού ➔ οι αλεπ ούδες OR οι αλεπ ούς . 2. Nouns that Refuse to Decline (Indeclinable) When Greek borrows words from English or French, it tries to fit them into the Greek system (e.g., Το σάντουιτς). But because they don't have Greek endings (-ος, -η, -ο), they are completely frozen. They are indeclinable . They rely entirely on the Article to show their case and number! Το σπορ (The sport - Nominative Singular) Βλέπω τα σπορ (I see the sports - Accusative Plural) Οι κανόνες των σπορ (The rules of the sports - Genitive Plural) Notice that the word σπορ never changed! 3. Defective Verbs (Missing Tenses) A "defective" verb is a verb that is missing an arm or a leg. It doesn't have a full set of tense conjugations. It only exists in specific forms. We already saw the most famous defective verbs in Unit 27: Impersonal Verbs . Words like πρέπει (must) and πρόκειται (is about to) only exist in the 3rd person singular. You cannot say "εγώ πρέπω" (I must). It doesn't exist! The "Past Only" Ghost There is a very cool, ancient verb that only exists in the Imperfect (Past Continuous) tense. It has no present, no future, and no simple past. Pre-existed / Was (Ήμουν / Υπήρχα) In C2 narrative writing or formal speech, you will often hear: - Επρόκειτο (It was about to / It was scheduled to). (This is the past tense of Πρόκειται. But you cannot make it Future or Simple Past!) 4. The Verb "Είδα" (I saw / I tell you!) You know the Aorist verb είδα (I saw). But in spoken Greek, the command form Ιδού (Behold / Here it is) is a defective remnant from Ancient Greek. It is only ever used as this one frozen word. Ιδού η Ρόδος, ιδού και το πήδημα! (Behold Rhodes, behold the jump - A famous ancient proverb meaning: "Stop bragging and prove it right here, right now!"). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that some masculine nouns change to Neuter in the plural ( ο χρόνος ➔ τα χρόνια ). [ ] I know that foreign loanwords ( το σπορ ) are indeclinable and rely purely on articles. [ ] I know that Defective Verbs are missing tenses or persons (like the impersonal πρέπει ).

masculine-declension.title: masculine-declension
masculine-declension.content: Masculine Declension (Κλίση Αρσενικών) Let's look at the "cheat codes" for Masculine nouns. Almost all masculine nouns end in -ος , -ας , or -ης . These are the three families of masculine words. Remember from our lesson on Cases: you only need to worry about the Nominative (Subject) and the Accusative (Object) for now. The Genitive (Possession) is included in the tables below for future reference. 1. Nouns ending in -ος This is the biggest and most famous family of masculine words. Example: ο φίλος (the friend) Case Singular English Nom (Subject) ο φίλ ος The friend is here. Acc (Object) το(ν) φίλ ο I see the friend . Gen (Owner) του φίλ ου The car of the friend . What happens? When the friend becomes the object (Accusative), two things happen: 1. The article ο changes to τον . 2. The noun φίλος drops its final -ς and becomes φίλο . Wait, why is it "το ν " sometimes and "το" other times? This is a pronunciation rule. You keep the 'N' if the next word starts with a vowel or a hard consonant ( κ, π, τ, μπ, ντ, γκ, τσ, τζ, ξ, ψ ). Otherwise, you drop the 'N'. Don't worry about memorizing this yet! 2. Nouns ending in -ας Example: ο πατέρας (the father) Case Singular Nom (Subject) ο πατέρ ας Acc (Object) το(ν) πατέρ α Gen (Owner) του πατέρ α What happens? It's the exact same rule! The article becomes τον , and the noun drops its final -ς . [!TIP] The "S" Rule The golden rule for almost all masculine nouns in the singular: If it's the subject, it keeps the 'S'. If it's receiving the action (Object/Accusative) or if it's the owner (Genitive), it drops the 'S'. 3. Nouns ending in -ης Example: ο χάρτης (the map) Case Singular Nom (Subject) ο χάρτ ης Acc (Object) το(ν) χάρτ η Gen (Owner) του χάρτ η What happens? You guessed it! The article changes to τον and the word drops its -ς . The Plural Forms When you want to talk about multiple friends/fathers/maps, the endings change again to show Plurality. Ending Plural Nominative (Subjects) Plural Accusative (Objects) -ος οι φίλ οι (the friends) τους φίλ ους (the friends) -ας οι πατέρ ες (the fathers) τους πατέρ ες (the fathers) -ης οι χάρτ ες (the maps) τους χάρτ ες (the maps) [!WARNING] Notice that for the -ας and -ης groups, the plural forms are exactly the same whether they are subjects or objects! - Οι πατέρες είναι εδώ. (The fathers are here). - Βλέπω τους πατέρες. (I see the fathers). The only thing that changes is the article ( οι ➔ τους ). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that masculine words almost always drop their final -ς when they are the Object (Accusative). [ ] I know that the article ο changes to τον . [ ] I know the plural article for subjects is οι , and for objects is τους .

negation.title: negation
negation.content: Negation (Η Άρνηση) You know how to say "I play" ( παίζω ). But what if you are tired and want to say "I don't play"? In English, we have to invent a helper word ("do") just to make a verb negative (I do not play). In Greek, making a sentence negative is incredibly simple. You just drop a single word right in front of the verb. That word is δεν (then) . 1. Using "Δεν" to Negate Verbs To say "not," you just stick δεν before the verb. It's like putting up a stop sign. παίζω (I play) ➔ δεν παίζω (I don't play) τρέχεις (You run) ➔ δεν τρέχεις (You don't run) έχουμε (We have) ➔ δεν έχουμε (We don't have) είναι (He is) ➔ δεν είναι (He isn't) You can do this with literally every verb in the Present tense. There are no exceptions! [!CAUTION] Wait, I thought "No" was "Όχι"? You probably learned that "yes" is ναι and "no" is όχι . - You use όχι to answer a question ("Do you want coffee? No.") - You use δεν to negate a verb ("I do not want coffee.") Never say "Όχι θέλω καφέ". That sounds like "No I want coffee." You must say "Δεν θέλω καφέ." 2. Pronunciation: The Disappearing 'N' Just like the article τον and την , the word δεν has a special pronunciation rule. When Greeks speak fast, they will often drop the 'N' at the end of δεν if the next word starts with a soft consonant. You don't need to try and copy this yet, but it's important to know so you understand what you are hearing! Δεν κάνω. ➔ The 'k' in κάνω is hard, so it stays δεν κάνω. Δεν μιλάω. ➔ The 'm' in μιλάω is soft, so Greeks will pronounce it as δε μιλάω. When you are writing, you should always write it as δεν to be safe. 3. The Negative Power Combo (Δεν + Weak Pronouns) In the previous unit, you learned that Weak pronouns (like σε for "you" or τον for "him") must be glued directly before the verb. What happens when δεν wants to jump in front of the verb too? Who wins? The pronoun always stays glued to the verb. The negation word δεν comes before the pronoun. Positive Sentence: Σε βλέπω. (I see you). Negative Sentence: Δεν σε βλέπω. (I don't see you). Positive Sentence: Ο Γιώργος την αγαπάει. (George loves her). Negative Sentence: Ο Γιώργος δεν την αγαπάει. (George doesn't love her). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that όχι means the standalone word "No". [ ] I know that δεν is used to make a verb negative (I do not play). [ ] I know that if there is a Weak pronoun ( σε, με, τον ), the δεν goes before it ( δεν σε βλέπω).

neuter-declension.title: neuter-declension
neuter-declension.content: Neuter Declension (Κλίση Ουδετέρων) Neuter nouns are the absolute easiest words to use in the Greek language. Why? Because their Nominative (Subject) and Accusative (Object) forms are exactly the same. They never change! Almost all neuter nouns end in -ο , -ι , or -μα . 1. Nouns ending in -ο Example: το βιβλίο (the book) Case Singular English Nom (Subject) το βιβλί ο The book is here. Acc (Object) το βιβλί ο I see the book . Gen (Owner) του βιβλί ου The pages of the book . What happens? Nothing! The article stays το , and the word stays βιβλίο . 2. Nouns ending in -ι Example: το παιδί (the child) Case Singular English Nom (Subject) το παιδ ί The child is here. Acc (Object) το παιδ ί I see the child . Gen (Owner) του παιδ ιού The toy of the child . What happens? Again, absolutely nothing! The Plural Forms When you want to talk about multiple books or children, the endings change to show Plurality. Here is the best part: just like in the singular, neuter nouns in the plural behave exactly the same whether they are subjects or objects! Ending Plural Nominative (Subjects) Plural Accusative (Objects) -ο τα βιβλί α (the books) τα βιβλί α (the books) -ι τα παιδ ιά (the children) τα παιδ ιά (the children) Look closely: - Τα βιβλία είναι εδώ. (The books are here - Nominative). - Βλέπω τα βιβλία . (I see the books - Accusative). - Τα παιδιά παίζουν. (The children are playing - Nominative). - Φωνάζω τα παιδιά . (I call the children - Accusative). [!TIP] The Golden Rule for Neuter Nouns In the singular, the article is always το . In the plural, the article is always τα AND the word almost always ends in -α . 3. The Special Case: Words in -μα We said almost all neuter nouns end in -ο or -ι. There is a third, very important group: those ending in -μα . Examples: - το πρόβλη μα (the problem) - το μάθη μα (the lesson) These are very easy in the singular (they don't change, just like the others). But in the plural, they grow an extra syllable: -τα . Case Singular Plural Nom (Subject) το πρόβλη μα τα προβλή ματα Acc (Object) το πρόβλη μα τα προβλή ματα 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that neuter singular nouns never change between being the Subject or the Object. The article is always το . [ ] I know that the plural article for neuter nouns is always τα , and the noun itself almost always ends in -α . [ ] I know that words ending in -μα add an extra -τα in the plural (το πρόβλημα ➔ τα προβλήματα).

nouns.title: Unit 2: The Basics of Nouns
nouns.content: Introduction to Nouns (Τα Ουσιαστικά) Congratulations on making it to Unit 2! You can now read Greek and pronounce words correctly. It's time to start building sentences. We will begin with Nouns —the words we use for people, places, and things. In English, nouns are incredibly simple. A "table" is just a table. In Greek, nouns are alive. They change their shape based on three things: Gender, Number, and Case . If this sounds intimidating, don't worry. We will tackle them one by one. The Concept of Grammatical Gender In English, objects don't have a gender. A boy is a "he," a girl is a "she," and a table is an "it." In Greek, every single noun has a gender , even inanimate objects. - A street is masculine (Ο). - A map is masculine (Ο). - A chair is feminine (Η). - A child is neuter (Το). [!WARNING] Don't try to find logic! There is no reason why a "chair" is female and a "street" is male. It is purely grammatical. You simply have to memorize the gender of a word when you learn it. How to Spot the Gender How do you know if a word is Masculine, Feminine, or Neuter? Greek is very generous here. 95% of the time, the last letter of the word gives away the gender. 1. Masculine Nouns (Ο) Almost all masculine words end in -ος , -ας , or -ης . Gender Class Word Meaning Ends in -ος ο φίλ ος (o fílos) the (male) friend Ends in -ας ο πατέρ ας (o patéras) the father Ends in -ης ο χάρτ ης (o chártis) the map 2. Feminine Nouns (Η) Almost all feminine words end in -η or -α . Gender Class Word Meaning Ends in -η η αγάπ η (i agápi) the love Ends in -α η μητέρ α (i mitéra) the mother 3. Neuter Nouns (Το) Almost all neuter words end in -ο , -ι , or -μα . Gender Class Word Meaning Ends in -ο το βιβλί ο (to vivlío) the book Ends in -ι το παιδ ί (to pedí) the child Ends in -μα το πρόβλη μα (to próvlima) the problem The Golden Rule of Greek Vocabulary Because gender dictates everything in Greek (how adjectives match the noun, how verbs behave), you must adhere to one iron-clad rule: [!IMPORTANT] The Golden Rule Never learn a Greek noun by itself. Always memorize it with its Definite Article (the word for "the" — ο, η, το ). Do not memorize that "house" is σπίτι . Memorize that it is το σπίτι . Do not memorize that "street" is δρόμος . Memorize that it is ο δρόμος . The article is the anchor that tells your brain how the rest of the sentence should behave. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I understand that every Greek noun belongs to one of three genders. [ ] I know that the ending of the word usually reveals its gender. [ ] I commit to never learning a noun without its article ( ο, η, το ). In the next lesson, we will dive deeper into those little words—the Articles!

object-pronouns.title: Unit 14: Object Pronouns
object-pronouns.content: Direct and Indirect Object Pronouns (Τα Αντικείμενα) In Unit 3, you learned about Direct Object Pronouns (me, you, him, her). These are words that receive the action directly. Τον βλέπω. (I see him ). Τις ξέρω. (I know them - the women). These are called "Direct" objects because they take the verb head-on. They are always in the Accusative Case (the 'N' case). But what happens when you have two objects in a sentence? 1. The Indirect Object (The Receiver) Imagine the sentence: "I give the book to him." There are two objects here: 1. The Book : This is what is being physically moved. This is the Direct Object . It goes in the Accusative Case ( το βιβλίο ). 2. Him : This is the person receiving the book. This is the Indirect Object . In Greek, the "Receiver" of an action (the person to whom something is given, told, or shown) goes into the Genitive Case . Wait, the Genitive Case? Isn't that for possession? Yes! The Genitive case does double duty in Greek. It marks the Owner (My book) AND it marks the Receiver (To me). Let's look at the Weak Genitive pronouns again: English Genitive Pronoun (Receiver) to me μου to you σου to him / to her / to it του / της / του to us μας to you (plural) σας to them τους Λέω ένα μυστικό. (I tell a secret - Direct Object) Σου λέω ένα μυστικό. (I tell you a secret - Indirect + Direct) 2. Order of Operations: Stacking Pronouns What if you want to replace both nouns with pronouns? Like instead of saying "I give the book to Maria", you want to say "I give it to her ." In Greek, the rule for stacking two pronouns is always the same: The Person (Genitive) comes first, and the Thing (Accusative) comes second. Who is receiving it? (της = to her) What is being received? (το = it) The Verb (δίνω = I give). ➔ Της το δίνω. (I give it to her.) Μου το λες; (Are you telling it to me ?) Σου τον δίνω. (I give him/it(masc) to you .) Ο Γιώργος της τα λέει. (George tells them(neut) to her .) 3. The Power of "Τους" Notice that the word τους appears in both the Accusative list (Unit 3) and the Genitive list (above). This makes τους a very powerful chameleon. You have to look at the verb to know what it means. If the verb takes a Direct Object (like "I see"): - Τους βλέπω = I see them (Accusative). If the verb takes an Indirect Object (like "I give"): - Τους δίνω = I give to them (Genitive). And what if you stack them together? (I give them [masc] to them ). ➔ Τους τους δίνω! (Yes, this is a real, correct Greek sentence!) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the Direct Object (the thing being acted on) takes the Accusative Case ( τον, την, το ). [ ] I know that the Indirect Object (the person receiving) takes the Genitive Case ( μου, σου, του ). [ ] I know that when stacking pronouns, the Person always comes before the Thing ( Μου το δίνεις).

participles.title: Unit 24: Active & Passive Participles
participles.content: Active & Passive Participles (Οι Μετοχές) A Participle is a verb that has been dressed up to act like an adjective or an adverb. In English, we make Participles by adding -ing (Running water) or -ed (The closed door). Greek does exactly the same thing. There are two types of Participles you need to master: Active and Passive . 1. The Active Participle (-οντας) The Active Participle is used to show that two actions are happening at the exact same time by the same person. In English, we usually translate this as " while [doing something] " or " by [doing something] ". To create the Active Participle, you take the Continuous (Present) stem of an active verb and add -οντας (or -ώντας for Type B verbs). τρέχω (I run) ➔ τρέχοντας (while running) διαβάζω (I read) ➔ διαβάζοντας (while reading) μιλάω (I speak - Type B) ➔ μιλώντας (while speaking) [!TIP] The Golden Rule of the Active Participle: It NEVER changes its ending. It doesn't care about gender, number, or case. It is completely indeclinable. It always ends in an 's' sound! Let's look at how it works in a sentence. Notice how it shows two things happening at once! Ο Γιώργος τρώει βλέποντας τηλεόραση. (George eats while watching TV.) Μαθαίνω ελληνικά ακούγοντας μουσική. (I learn Greek by listening to music.) 2. The Passive Participle (-μένος) The Passive Participle is completely different. It acts exactly like a normal Adjective. In English, this is the " -ed " or " -en " ending (The broken glass, the hidden door, the tired man). It describes the final state of an object after an action has been done to it. To create the Passive Participle, you take the Passive stem and add -μένος, -μένη, -μένο . Because it acts like an adjective, it is a chameleon! It must match the noun it describes in Gender, Number, and Case. κουράζω (I tire) ➔ κουρασμένος (tired) κλείνω (I close) ➔ κλεισμένος (closed) κρύβω (I hide) ➔ κρυμμένος (hidden) Look at how it behaves exactly like a normal adjective: Η Μαρία είναι πολύ κουρασμένη σήμερα. (Maria is very tired today.) (Matches Feminine Singular: κουρασμένη). Βλέπω τις κλειδωμένες πόρτες. (I see the locked doors.) (Matches Feminine Plural Accusative: κλειδωμένες). 3. The Ultimate Difference It is crucial to understand the difference between the two participles. Active Participle (-οντας): "While doing X". Describes an ongoing action . Never changes ending. Passive Participle (-μένος): "Having been X'd". Describes a completed state . Acts like an adjective and changes ending. Κλαίγοντας (while crying) vs Κλαμένος (tear-stained / having cried) Κρύβοντας (while hiding something) vs Κρυμμένος (hidden) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the Active Participle ( -οντας ) translates to "while doing" or "by doing". [ ] I know that the Active Participle is frozen and never changes its ending. [ ] I know that the Passive Participle ( -μένος ) translates to the "-ed" adjective form (tired, closed). [ ] I know that the Passive Participle acts like an adjective and must match the noun it describes.

particles.title: Unit 30: Nuanced Particles
particles.content: Nuanced Particles (Μόρια με Σημασία) Back in Level A, you learned about grammatical particles: tiny words that don't change, but trigger grammar rules (like θα for the future, or να for the subjunctive). In Level C1, you need to learn about Conversational Particles . These are tiny, untranslatable words that Greeks sprinkle into their sentences to add flavor, doubt, sarcasm, or intense emotion. Dropping these into a conversation immediately signals that you are an advanced speaker. 1. Άραγε (I wonder...) Άραγε (Á-ra-ge) is used to express curiosity, doubt, or wondering to yourself. It is usually placed at the beginning or the very end of a question. There's no direct translation in English, but it acts like the phrase "I wonder..." or "Could it be that...?" Άραγε θα έρθει η Μαρία στο πάρτι; (I wonder if Maria will come to the party). Τι σκέφτεται άραγε ; (What is he thinking, I wonder?) Πού είναι άραγε τα κλειδιά μου; (Where on earth are my keys?) 2. Μακάρι (If only / Let's hope) Μακάρι (Ma-ká-ri) comes from an ancient word meaning "blessed". Today, it is used to express a strong, almost desperate wish or hope. It is heavily used in daily Greek. It usually triggers the Subjunctive ( να ). Μακάρι να περάσω τις εξετάσεις! (If only I pass the exams! / Let's hope I pass!) Μακάρι να είχα λεφτά... (If only I had money... [Type 2 Conditional Dream!]) You can also use it completely on its own as a response! - Person A: Θα βρούμε ωραίο καιρό αύριο; (Will we have nice weather tomorrow?) - Person B: Μακάρι! (Let's hope so!) 3. Τάχα & Δήθεν (Supposedly / "Yeah, right") These two particles are used to show extreme sarcasm, doubt, or that you believe someone is lying. They both loosely translate to "supposedly" or "allegedly", but with a strong rolling of the eyes. Τάχα (Tá-ha) and Δήθεν (Dí-then) can be used interchangeably. Κλαίει δήθεν επειδή χτύπησε. (He is supposedly crying because he got hurt [but I know he is faking it]). Μου είπε τάχα ότι ήταν άρρωστος. (He told me allegedly that he was sick [I don't believe him for a second]). If someone tells you an obvious lie, you can just respond: " Ναι, τάχα μου! " (Yeah, right!). 4. Ας (Let's / Let it be) You learned the command forms in Unit 19 (Do it!). But what if you want to say " Let's do it" or " Let him do it"? You use the particle Ας (As). It triggers the Subjunctive (no 'να' needed, 'ας' does the job directly). Ας πάμε! ( Let's go!) Ας μιλήσει ο Γιώργος. ( Let George speak). Ας είναι. ( Let it be / Whatever). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I can express "I wonder" by using Άραγε in a question. [ ] I can express strong hope or wishes using Μακάρι . [ ] I can show sarcasm or disbelief using Τάχα or Δήθεν (supposedly). [ ] I know how to say "Let's..." using the particle Ας + Verb.

passive-voice.title: Unit 22: True Passive Voice
passive-voice.content: True Passive Voice (Η Παθητική Φωνή) In Unit 18, we met Deponent Verbs (Type C). These are verbs that look passive (they end in -ομαι ) but have an active meaning ("I come", "I need"). Now it is time to look at the True Passive Voice . This is when you take a normal, active verb (like "I wash") and flip it around so that the subject is receiving the action ("I am washed"). 1. Creating the Present Passive How do you turn an Active verb into a Passive verb? You drop the active ending ( -ω ) and attach the passive ending ( -ομαι )! Let's use the verb πλένω (I wash). Active: Πλένω το φλιτζάνι. (I wash the cup). Passive: Το φλιτζάνι πλένεται . (The cup is washed). You already learned these endings in Unit 18! Let's review them: Pronoun The Passive Ending Example (πλένομαι - I am washed ) (Εγώ) -ομαι πλέν ομαι (Εσύ) -εσαι πλέν εσαι (Αυτό/ή/ό) -εται πλέν εται (Εμείς) -όμαστε πλεν όμαστε (Εσείς) -εστε πλέν εστε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ονται πλέν ονται [!TIP] The Reflexive Trick In Greek, the Passive Voice doesn't just mean an action is done to you by someone else. It is also used to show actions you do to yourself (Reflexive). - Πλένω το παιδί = I wash the child. (Active) - Πλένομαι = I wash myself / I take a bath. (Passive/Reflexive) - Σηκώνω το κουτί = I lift the box. (Active) - Σηκώνομαι = I lift myself / I stand up. (Passive/Reflexive) 2. Who did it? (The word "από") When a sentence is passive ("The house was built"), you can choose to include the person who actually did the building ("The house was built by George"). In Greek, the word for "by" in a passive sentence is από (from). And as you learned in the last lesson, 'από' is always followed by the Accusative Case! Το βιβλίο διαβάζεται από τον Γιώργο. (The book is read by George.) 3. Creating the Past Passive (Aorist) Here is where things get completely new. You know how to make an Active verb past tense (γράφω ➔ έγραψα ). But how do you say "It was written"? The Passive Aorist has its own unique set of rules and its own unique set of endings. Rule 1: The Theta (Θ) The hallmark of the Passive Past is the letter Θ (Theta). The vast majority of passive past verbs have a 'θ' right before the ending. Rule 2: The Endings The endings for the Passive Aorist are: -ηκα, -ηκες, -ηκε, -ήκαμε, -ήκατε, -ηκαν . Let's look at the verb πλένω (I wash) in the Passive Past (I was washed). 1. Drop the -ω. (πλέν-) 2. Add the Theta. (πλέν-θ... wait, 'νθ' is hard to say. The 'ν' drops out! ➔ πλύθ- (Yes, sometimes the vowels change too!) ) 3. Add the Augment (έ-) and the new endings! Pronoun The Past Passive Ending Example (πλύθηκα - I was washed ) (Εγώ) -ηκα πλύθ ηκα (Εσύ) -ηκες πλύθ ηκες (Αυτό/ή/ό) -ηκε πλύθ ηκε (Εμείς) -ήκαμε πλυθ ήκαμε (Εσείς) -ήκατε πλυθ ήκατε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ηκαν πλύθ ηκαν (Note: These endings already start with an 'η' which is a vowel, so you only need to add an extra 'έ-' augment if the verb root itself is extremely short). Ο καφές φτιάχτηκε από τη Μαρία. (The coffee was made by Maria.) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know how to turn an Active verb ( πλένω ) into a Present Passive verb ( πλένομαι ). [ ] I know that Passive verbs are also used reflexively (Things I do to myself, like wash/stand up). [ ] I know that the agent (the person doing the action) is introduced by από . [ ] I know that the Past Passive (Aorist) is characterized by the letter Θ and the endings -ηκα, -ηκες, -ηκε .

past-conjugation.title: past-conjugation
past-conjugation.content: Past Tense Conjugation In Modern Greek, there are two main past tenses: the Imperfect (actions that were happening) and the Aorist (actions that happened once). They use different stems and endings. Imperfect (Παρατατικός) "I was playing" Εγώ έπαιζ-α Εσύ έπαιζ-ες Αυτός έπαιζ-ε Εμείς παίζ-αμε Εσείς παίζ-ατε Αυτοί έπαιζ-αν Aorist (Αόριστος) "I played" Εγώ έπαιξ-α Εσύ έπαιξ-ες Αυτός έπαιξ-ε Εμείς παίξ-αμε Εσείς παίξ-ατε Αυτοί έπαιξ-αν The Augment (Το Πρόσφυμα) Did you notice the extra έ- at the beginning of some forms? This is called the Augment . It is added to past tenses when the verb has two syllables, making it three. In plural forms like παίζαμε , the stress moves, and the augment usually disappears.

perfect-tenses.title: Unit 23: The Perfect Tenses
perfect-tenses.content: The Perfect Tenses (Οι Συντελεσμένοι Χρόνοι) In Level B1, you learned how to talk about the Past in two ways: 1. The Imperfect (I used to play / I was playing) - Continuous 2. The Aorist (I played) - Simple But what if you want to say " I have played "? This is a very specific type of past. It means you did something in the past, but the result is still important right now. This is called the Present Perfect Tense (Παρακείμενος), and it is the first of the three "Perfect" tenses. Good news: The Perfect tenses in Greek are formed exactly like they are in English! 1. The Helper Verb (Έχω) In English, to make a Perfect tense, you use the helper verb "have" + a past participle: "I have eaten." Greek does the exact same thing. You use the verb έχω (I have), conjugated for whoever is doing the action. (Εγώ) έχω (Εσύ) έχεις (Αυτός/ή/ό) έχει (Εμείς) έχουμε (Εσείς) έχετε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) έχουν 2. The Unchanging Ending (-ει) After the helper verb, you need the actual action word (like "eaten", "written", "played"). In Greek, this word is created using the Simple Stem (the Aorist stem you learned in Unit 16) + the ending -ει . And here is the magic: This word never, ever changes. It doesn't care if you are a man, a woman, plural, or singular. It is frozen in time. Let's use the verb παίζω ➔ Simple Stem: παίξ- : The frozen word is παίξει . Pronoun The Formula (Helper + Frozen Word) English (Εγώ) έχω παίξει I have played (Εσύ) έχεις παίξει You have played (Αυτό/ή/ό) έχει παίξει He/She/It has played (Εμείς) έχουμε παίξει We have played (Εσείς) έχετε παίξει You have played (Αυτοί/ές/ά) έχουν(ε) παίξει They have played Έχω διαβάσει αυτό το βιβλίο. (I have read this book.) Ο Γιώργος έχει αγοράσει ένα αυτοκίνητο. (George has bought a car.) 3. The Past Perfect (I had played) What if you want to say "I had played"? (Before something else happened). In English, you just change the helper verb to the past tense ("have" ➔ "had"). In Greek, you do exactly the same thing! Change "έχω" to its continuous past tense ( είχα ). The frozen word ( παίξει ) stays exactly the same! Pronoun The Past Perfect English (Εγώ) είχα παίξει I had played (Εσύ) είχες παίξει You had played (Αυτός/ή/ό) είχε παίξει He had played (Εμείς) είχαμε παίξει We had played (Εσείς) είχατε παίξει You had played (Αυτοί/ές/ά) είχαν παίξει They had played 4. The Future Perfect (I will have played) What if you want to say "I will have played" by tomorrow? Just make the helper verb future tense! ( θα έχω ). Again, the frozen word never changes! Pronoun The Future Perfect English (Εγώ) θα έχω παίξει I will have played (Εσύ) θα έχεις παίξει You will have played (Αυτός/ή/ό) θα έχει παίξει He will have played (And so on...) Μέχρι αύριο, θα έχω τελειώσει τη δουλειά μου. (By tomorrow, I will have finished my work.) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the Perfect Tenses are built using the helper verb έχω (I have). [ ] I know how to make the frozen action word ( Simple Stem + ει ). [ ] I know that I can change the tense by just changing the helper verb: Present Perfect: Έχω γράψει (I have written) Past Perfect: Είχα γράψει (I had written) Future Perfect: Θα έχω γράψει (I will have written)

personal-pronouns.title: personal-pronouns
personal-pronouns.content: Personal Pronouns (Προσωπικές Αντωνυμίες) In the lesson on "Είμαι" (I am), you learned the Subject Pronouns (I, You, He, She, We, They). You also learned the Pro-Drop Rule : Greeks almost never use these words, because the verb ending tells you who is doing the action! So when do we use pronouns? We use them when people are the Object of the sentence. 1. Object Pronouns: The Receivers Just like in English, when a pronoun stops being the "Doer" (Nominative) and becomes the "Receiver" (Accusative), the word changes. English : I see him. He sees me . Greek : Εγώ τον βλέπω. Αυτός βλέπει εμένα . In Greek, Object Pronouns come in two flavors: Strong and Weak . 2. Weak Pronouns (The Normal Way) This is the default way to say "me," "you," "him," etc. in Greek. These words are tiny (usually just two letters). They are so weak that they cannot stand on their own. They must be glued directly in front of the verb. English Weak Pronoun Example Translation Me με Με βλέπεις; Do you see me ? You σε Σε θέλω. I want you . Him τον Τον ξέρω. I know him . Her τη(ν) Την αγαπώ. I love her . It το Το έχω. I have it . Us μας Μας ακούνε. They hear us . You (pl) σας Σας ευχαριστώ. I thank you . Them (m) τους Τους βλέπω. I see them (the men). Them (f) τις Τις βλέπω. I see them (the women). Them (n) τα Τα βλέπω. I see them (the kids/things). [!CAUTION] Word Order Warning! In English, the pronoun comes after the verb: "I love you ". In Greek, the weak pronoun MUST come before the verb: " Σε αγαπώ" (Literally: "You I love"). This feels backwards at first, but your brain will get used to it very quickly! 3. Strong Pronouns (The Dramatic Way) Sometimes, you need to emphasize who is receiving the action. Maybe you want to contrast two people, or maybe you are yelling! For this, Greek uses Strong Pronouns . These are longer words, and they are independent—you can put them wherever you want in the sentence, just like in English. English Strong Pronoun ME! εμένα YOU! εσένα HIM! αυτόν HER! αυτή(ν) IT! αυτό US! εμάς YOU ALL! εσάς THEM! αυτούς (m) / αυτές (f) / αυτά (n) When to use Strong Pronouns? Only use them when you want to add bold, dramatic emphasis. Situation A: Normal Conversation - "Do you love me?" - Ναι, σε αγαπώ. (Yes, I love you). [Weak pronoun used] Situation B: The Soap Opera - "Do you love Maria?!" - Όχι! Αγαπώ εσένα ! (No! I love YOU !) [Strong pronoun used] Notice that in Situation B, the strong pronoun εσένα was placed after the verb, exactly like in English. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that Greeks drop the Subject Pronoun (I/You/We) 90% of the time. [ ] I know that Weak Object Pronouns (me, you, him) MUST go directly before the verb (e.g., Σε βλέπω). [ ] I know that Strong Pronouns (εμένα, εσένα) are only used for dramatic emphasis.

phrases.title: phrases
phrases.content: Essential Phrases Mastering these core phrases will help you navigate your first interactions in Greek. Practice saying them out loud to improve your pronunciation! Greetings Γειά σου! Hello / Hi (informal) Γειά σας! Hello (formal/plural) Καλημέρα! Good morning! Καλησπέρα! Good evening! Basic Interaction Τι κάνεις; How are you? Καλά είμαι. I am well. Πώς σε λένε; What is your name? Με λένε... My name is... Courtesy Ευχαριστώ. Thank you. Παρακαλώ. Please / You're welcome. Συγγνώμη. I'm sorry / Excuse me. Ναι / Όχι Yes / No Did you know? The word Παρακαλώ is extremely versatile. It means "please", but also "you're welcome", and sometimes "I'm sorry?" or "Yes, can I help you?".

plurals.title: Unit 8: Plural Nouns & Adjectives
plurals.content: Plural Nouns & Adjectives (Ο Πληθυντικός) Welcome to the A2 Stage! You have mastered the singular forms of nouns, adjectives, and verbs. Now it is time to multiply EVERYTHING. In Unit 2, we peeked at the plural articles: - οι (The - for Masc & Fem subjects) - τους (The - for Masc objects) - τις (The - for Fem objects) - τα (The - for Neuter everything) Now let's see what happens to the actual nouns and adjectives. 1. Masculine Plurals (Οι, Τους) Masculine words are the most dramatic. They change their ending completely. Singular Root: -ος, -ας, -ης Plural Root: -οι , -ες Dictionary Form (Singular) Plural Subject (Nom) Plural Object (Acc) ο φίλος (the friend) ο ι φίλ οι το υς φίλ ους ο μαύρος (the black...) ο ι μαύρ οι το υς μαύρ ους ο πατέρας (the father) ο ι πατέρ ες το υς πατέρ ες ο μαθητής (the student) ο ι μαθητ ές το υς μαθητ ές [!WARNING] The Tricky "-οι" Ending Look closely at the top row. Both the article ( οι ) and the noun ( φίλοι ) end in an "i" sound. But when they become the Object, the article changes to τους , and the noun ending changes to -ους ! - Οι καλοί φίλοι είναι εδώ. (The good friends are here.) - Βλέπω τους καλούς φίλους . (I see the good friends.) 2. Feminine Plurals (Οι, Τις) Feminine words are wonderful because their plural endings never change between Subject and Object . The noun stays exactly the same; only the article changes! Singular Root: -η, -α Plural Root: -ες Dictionary Form (Singular) Plural Subject (Nom) Plural Object (Acc) η μητέρα (the mother) ο ι μητέρ ες τ ις μητέρ ες η καλή (the good...) ο ι καλ ές τ ις καλ ές η κόρη (the daughter) ο ι κόρ ες τ ις κόρ ες η ώρα (the hour) ο ι ώρ ες τ ις ώρ ες Οι όμορφες γυναίκες είναι εδώ. (The beautiful women are here.) Βλέπω τις όμορφες γυναίκες . (I see the beautiful women.) Notice how nothing changed except the article (Οι ➔ Τις)! 3. Neuter Plurals (Τα) Neuter words are the ultimate lazy words. Not only do their plural endings never change between Subject and Object, but their plural article (τα) never changes either! Singular Root: -ο, -ι, -μα Plural Root: -α , -ια , -ματα Dictionary Form (Singular) Plural Subject (Nom) Plural Object (Acc) το βιβλίο (the book) τ α βιβλί α τ α βιβλί α το καλό (the good...) τ α καλ ά τ α καλ ά το παιδί (the child) τ α παιδ ιά τ α παιδ ιά το μάθημα (the lesson) τ α μαθή ματα τ α μαθή ματα 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist Look at the sentence below and see if you can identify why every single word is spelled the way it is: Οι καλοί πατέρες βλέπουν τα μικρά παιδιά. (The good fathers see the small children.) Οι καλοί : Masculine plural subjects (-οι pattern). πατέρες : Masculine plural subject (-ας becomes -ες). βλέπουν : 3rd person plural verb ("They" see). τα μικρά : Neuter plural object. παιδιά : Neuter plural object (-ι becomes -ια).

possessive-pronouns.title: possessive-pronouns
possessive-pronouns.content: Possessive Pronouns (Κτητικές Αντωνυμίες) In the last lesson, we learned how to say "George's dog" using the Genitive case (Ο σκύλος του Γιώργου ). But what if you don't want to use a name? What if you just want to say " My dog" or " His dog"? For this, we use the Weak Pronouns you learned back in Unit 3! (με, σε, τον, την..). But we use the Genitive version of them. 1. The Possessive Words (My, Your, His...) These are tiny, little words that mean "my", "your", "his", etc. English Greek My μου Your σου His / Her / Its του / της / του Our μας Your (plural) σας Their τους The Golden Rule of Position In English, the possessive word comes before the noun (My car). In Greek, the possessive word must come AFTER the noun! (The car my). Το αυτοκίνητό μου (My car) Ο σκύλος σου (Your dog) Το πρόβλημά μας (Our problem) Η αδερφή του (His sister) Η μητέρα της (Her mother) [!CAUTION] Who is doing the matching? The word "του" means his , and the word "της" means hers . This word ONLY cares about the gender of the Owner , not the object being owned! - Ο σκύλος της (Her dog) ➔ The dog is masculine, but the owner is a woman! - Η γάτα του (His cat) ➔ The cat is feminine, but the owner is a man! 2. Emphatic Possession (Mine!) Sometimes, saying "my car" isn't enough. Someone is trying to steal your car, and you want to yell: "That is my own car!" or "That car is mine !" For this, we use a special word: Δικός (di-kós). Because δικός is an adjective, it is a chameleon. It must match the gender of the object being owned . And because you still need to say whose it is, you combine it with the little words from above: Ο δικός μου σκύλος (My own dog - Masc) Η δική μου γάτα (My own cat - Fem) Το δικό μου σπίτι (My own house - Neut) Situation: Whose coffee is this? Normal Answer: Είναι ο καφές μου . (It is my coffee.) Emphatic Answer: Είναι ο δικός μου (καφές)! (It is MINE !) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know the possessive words ( μου, σου, του, της, μας, σας, τους ). [ ] I know that these words must go after the noun (Το βιβλίο μου ). [ ] I know that to say "Mine!" or "My own", I use the chameleon word δικός/δική/δικό + μου/σου/του.

prepositions.title: Unit 21: Prepositions in Depth
prepositions.chapter: 3. B1 The Great Divide: Verbal Aspect
prepositions.content: Prepositions in Depth (Οι Προθέσεις) Prepositions are tiny words that show relationships in space, time, or logic. You already know the "Big Three" from Unit 5: σε (to/at/in), από (from), and με (with). You also learned how σε merges with the Definite Articles: - σε + τον = στον - σε + την = στην - σε + το = στο For the entirety of Level A (A1 and A2), we taught you that prepositions are always followed by the Accusative Case (the Object Case). Now that you are in Level B1, it is time to look a little deeper. 1. The Standard Group (Accusative Case) The vast majority of everyday Greek prepositions take the Accusative Case . Whenever you see these words, the noun that follows them will drop its '-ς' (if masculine) and take Accusative articles (τον/την/το). Preposition Meaning Example σε (στον/στην/στο) to / at / in Πάω στην Ελλάδα. από from / by / than Έρχομαι από τη δουλειά. με with Μιλάω με τον Γιώργο. για for / about Αυτό είναι για σένα. χωρίς without Καφές χωρίς ζάχαρη. μέχρι / ως until / as far as Δουλεύω μέχρι αύριο. προς towards Πάμε προς το κέντρο. μετά (από) after Θα φάμε μετά το μάθημα. πριν (από) before Πλύσου πριν το φαγητό. English: This letter is for the teacher. Greek: Αυτό το γράμμα είναι για τον δάσκαλο. (The masculine subject "ο δάσκαλος" becomes "τον δάσκαλο" after "για"). 2. The Advanced Group (Genitive Case) Here is the twist. There is a smaller group of prepositions that demand the Genitive Case (the Owner Case - του/της/του ). Most of these are formal words or remnants from Ancient Greek (which you will learn more about in C2 Mastery). However, several of them are used in everyday spoken Greek. Preposition Meaning Example υπέρ in favor of / for Είμαι υπέρ της αλλαγής. (I am in favor of the change.) κατά against Είμαι κατά του πολέμου. (I am against the war.) εκτός (από) except Όλοι ήρθαν εκτός από του Γιώργου. (Actually, εκτός από takes the Accusative in modern Greek: εκτός από τον Γιώργο! Let's strike that...) Let's make this easier. The true Genitive prepositions are mostly spatial adverbs that have evolved to take the Genitive. Spatial Adverbs + Genitive These are incredibly common. They express location relative to something else. δίπλα μου (next to me) μπροστά του (in front of him) πίσω της (behind her) ανάμεσά μας (between us) απέναντί τους (opposite them / across from them) Notice how they use the Weak Possessive Pronouns (μου, σου, του) from Unit 13! If you want to use a noun instead of a pronoun, you still use the Genitive Case! - πίσω από το σπίτι (Accusative - This is one way to do it!) - πίσω του σπιτιού (Genitive - This is the other way to do it!) Wait, let's keep it simple for B1. The Golden Rule for B1: Unless you are using a Weak Pronoun (μου/σου/του) directly attached to a spatial word (δίπλα μου), use the Accusative Case for EVERYTHING. next to the park ➔ δίπλα στο πάρκο. behind the house ➔ πίσω από το σπίτι. opposite the bank ➔ απέναντι από την τράπεζα. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I have memorized the major prepositions ( σε, από, με, για, χωρίς, μέχρι, προς, πριν, μετά ). [ ] I know that almost all prepositions are followed by the Accusative Case (τον/την/το). [ ] I know that spatial words like δίπλα (next to) or πίσω (behind) can take weak Genitive pronouns ( δίπλα μου ), or they use σε/από + Accusative for nouns ( δίπλα στο πάρκο ).

pronouns.title: pronouns
pronouns.content: Intro to Pronouns Pronouns are words used in place of nouns to avoid repetition and make speech more fluid. Modern Greek has several categories of pronouns, most of which decline based on gender, number, and case—just like the nouns they replace. Personal Pronouns I, you, he, she, it... Possessive Pronouns My, your, his, hers... Demonstrative Pronouns This, that, these, those... Relative Pronouns Who, which, that... Key Rule: Omission In Modern Greek, subject pronouns (I, you, we) are often omitted because the verb ending already tells us who is performing the action. We only use them for emphasis or clarity. Διαβάζω. (I read) vs Εγώ διαβάζω. ( I [emphasized] read)

punctuation.title: punctuation
punctuation.content: Punctuation Marks (Σημεία Στίξης) You have reached the final piece of the modern Greek writing system: Punctuation. Good news—Greek punctuation is almost identical to English! There are only two major differences you need to memorize, and they are both guaranteed to trip you up at least once. 1. The Greek Question Mark (;) This is the most notorious "gotcha" in the entire language. In Greek, the English semicolon (;) is used as the question mark. The English question mark (?) does not exist in Greek at all. If you type it, Greeks will be very confused! English : How are you? Greek : Πώς είσαι ; (Pós íse?) [!TIP] Typing Tip If you have a Greek keyboard installed on your phone or computer, you don't need to do anything special. Just hit the key where the "Q" key usually is (on mobile) or the standard semicolon key (on desktop). 2. The Ano Teleia (·) Because Greek stole the semicolon to use as a question mark, what do they use when they actually want a semicolon (a pause between two related sentences)? They use the Ano Teleia (Άνω Τελεία), which translates to "Upper Dot". It looks exactly like a period that has floated up to the middle of the line: · It functions exactly like an English semicolon (;) or a colon (:). English : I want water; I am thirsty. Greek : Θέλω νερό · διψάω. [!NOTE] Don't Stress About Typing It The Ano Teleia is very rare in casual texting or daily writing. Most Greeks just use a comma or a period instead because the · symbol is physically hard to type on most keyboards! You just need to recognize it when reading books or articles. 3. The Quotation Marks (« ») Greek uses the exact same period ( . ), comma ( , ), and exclamation mark ( ! ) as English. However, when quoting speech or a title, Greek traditionally uses angular quotation marks , also known as guillemets . English : He said "hello". Greek : Είπε « γεια » . In modern digital typing, standard English quotation marks (" ") are becoming more common, but you will still see the angular ones in all printed books and news articles. 🎯 A1 Unit 1 Checklist Congratulations! You have officially mastered the mechanics of the Greek language. You can now sound out any word, know where to put the stress, and understand the punctuation. Let's do a final check. Do you know: - [ ] The English sounds for all 24 letters? - [ ] That αυ and ευ make a "v" or "f" sound? - [ ] That μπ = B, ντ = D, and γκ = G? - [ ] That ; is a question mark? If you answered yes to all of these, you are ready to start building actual sentences in Unit 2 !

read.title: read
read.content: Learn to Read: The "Phonetic Lego" System Now that you know the Alphabet (Single sounds) and the Digraphs (Double Sounds), you have all the tools you need to read 99% of modern Greek. Unlike English, where "tough", "though", and "through" all look similar but sound completely different, Greek is highly phonetic . Once you memorize the sound a letter or combination makes, it almost never changes. It is like assembling a Lego set. Syllabic Assembly Greek words sound exactly like they are spelled. To read Greek, mentally break every word down into syllables, normally stopping after a vowel. Example 1: Easy Syllables Let's build the word for "mother". μα (ma) + μά (ma) ➔ μαμά (ma-má) = mom Example 2: Mixing Single and Double Letters Let's build the word for "sky". It contains the double vowel ου (oo) . ου (oo) + ρα (ra) + νός (nós) ➔ ουρανός (oo-ra-nós) = sky Example 3: The Danger Zone (False Friends) Let's build the word for "water". It looks like the English word "vepo". Let's prove why it's not. - ν = n - ε = e - ρ = r (Rolled R, not a P!) - ό = o νε (ne) + ρό (ró) ➔ νερό (ne-ró) = water [!WARNING] If you struggle with reading early on, read out loud . Say every syllable deliberately: ne... ro . oo... ra... nos . It builds the mental bridge much faster than reading silently. The Accent Mark (Τονισμός) Did you notice the little dash mark over the vowels in the examples above? ( μά , νός , ρό ). This is called the tonos . In English, you just have to "know" which syllable to stress. (e.g. " re cord" the noun vs "re cord " the verb). In Greek, the accent mark tells you exactly where to put the stress. Every word with two or more syllables must have exactly one accent mark. Practice: Following the Stress Look at the word for "problem": πρόβλημα (pró-vli-ma) . The stress is on the first syllable ( ό ). Now look at word for "math": μαθηματικά (ma-thi-ma-ti-ká) . The stress is all the way on the very last syllable ( ά ). 🎯 Final Review If you can remember that: 1. Η, Υ, Ι, ΕΙ, ΟΙ all make the exact same [ee] sound 2. Β makes a [v] sound 3. Ρ makes an [r] sound 4. You have to hit the syllable with the accent mark... ...you will be reading Greek fluently in a matter of weeks!

reflexive-pronouns.title: reflexive-pronouns
reflexive-pronouns.content: Reflexive Pronouns Reflexive pronouns are used when the subject and the object of a verb are the same person. In Greek, we use the phrase ο εαυτός μου (my self). 1st Person ο εαυτός μου myself 2nd Person ο εαυτός σου yourself 3rd Person ο εαυτός του / της himself / herself Plural Reflexives In the plural, the word "self" (εαυτός) can either stay singular or become plural (εαυτοί). Person Singular (Self) Plural (Selves) 1st (Ourselves) τον εαυτό μας τους εαυτούς μας 2nd (Yourselves) τον εαυτό σας τους εαυτούς σας 3rd (Themselves) τον εαυτό τους τους εαυτούς τους Common Usage Reflexive pronouns are usually used as objects. Example: Σκέφτομαι τον εαυτό μου. (I am thinking of myself).

relative-pronouns.title: Unit 26: Relative Pronouns
relative-pronouns.content: Relative Pronouns (Αναφορικές Αντωνυμίες) A relative pronoun is a word like "who", "which", or "that". It connects two sentences together so you don't have to repeat the same noun twice. The man is tall. The man is my brother. The man who is tall is my brother. In Greek, there are two main ways to do this: the easy way, and the formal way. 1. The Magic Word: Που (That / Who / Which) This is the easiest, most common, and most useful word in the modern Greek language. Που means "that", "which", or "who". And the best part? It never changes. It doesn't care about gender, number, or case. It is completely frozen. Ο άντρας που βλέπεις είναι ο αδερφός μου. (The man who you see is my brother). Το βιβλίο που διαβάζω είναι καλό. (The book which I am reading is good). Η γυναίκα που μένει εδώ έφυγε. (The woman that lives here left). [!WARNING] Spelling Alert: που vs πού Do not confuse the relative pronoun που (no accent) with the question word πού (with accent). - Το σπίτι που μένω. (The house that I live in.) - Πού μένεις; ( Where do you live?) 2. The Formal Way: Ο Οποίος (Who / Which) While που is perfect for 90% of daily conversations, you will absolutely encounter its formal cousin: Ο οποίος (o o-pí-os). You will see this in the news, in books, in official documents, and when people want to sound very polite or precise. Unlike "που", ο οποίος is a chameleon. Both the article AND the pronoun must match the noun they are replacing in Gender, Number, and Case! Masculine: Ο οποίος Feminine: Η οποία Neuter: Το οποίο Που vs Ο οποίος (Nominative Subject) Η δασκάλα που μιλάει είναι καλή. (Everyday) Η δασκάλα η οποία μιλάει είναι καλή. (Formal) (Both mean: The teacher who is speaking is good). When MUST you use "ο οποίος"? There is one huge grammatical rule where you cannot use "που". If the relative pronoun comes immediately after a Preposition (like με, από, για ), you are totally banned from using "που". You must use the matching form of ο οποίος (usually in the Accusative form: τον οποίο / την οποία / το οποίο ). Η κοπέλα με την οποία μιλάω. (The girl with whom I am speaking). Το πρόβλημα για το οποίο ξέρεις. (The problem about which you know). (In spoken Greek, people often sidestep this by just ditching the relative pronoun entirely and rewording the sentence: "Η κοπέλα που μιλάω μαζί της" [The girl that I speak with her]. But grammatically, the "ο οποίος" structure is the correct one!) 3. Other Useful Relatives Here are three more relative pronouns you will hear all the time. They all act like adjectives and must match their nouns! Greek Meaning Example Όποιος (-α, -ο) Whoever / Whichever Όποιος θέλει, ας έρθει. (Whoever wants, let him come). Όσος (-η, -ο) As much/many as Πάρε όσα λεφτά θέλεις. (Take as much money as you want). Ό,τι Whatever Φάε ό,τι θέλεις! (Eat whatever you want!) [!CAUTION] Ό,τι vs Ότι Notice the comma inside the word ό,τι ! - Ό,τι (with a comma) means "Whatever". - Ότι (without a comma) means "That" (as a conjunction, e.g., "I know that you are here" - Ξέρω ότι είσαι εδώ). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the frozen word που means "who, which, or that" and is used in 90% of daily speech. [ ] I know that the formal version is Ο οποίος/Η οποία/Το οποίο and must match the noun. [ ] I know that I must use "ο οποίος" after a preposition (με την οποία). [ ] I have memorized the special words Όποιος (Whoever), Όσος (As much as), and Ό,τι (Whatever).

simple-future.title: Unit 17: Simple Future & Subjunctive
simple-future.chapter: 3. B1 The Great Divide: Verbal Aspect
simple-future.content: The Simple Future (Ο Συνοπτικός Μέλλοντας) In Level A2 (Unit 11), you learned the Continuous Future . You used it to talk about ongoing habits or repeated actions in the future ("I will be playing every day"). To make the Continuous Future, you took the Present tense verb and put the word θα in front of it: Θα παίζω. But what if you don't want to play every day? What if you just want to play a single game of tennis tomorrow? You need the Simple Future Tense . 1. The Simple Stem Remember the rule of Aspect from Unit 15? - The Continuous Aspect is built on the Present Stem . - The Simple Aspect is built on the Aorist Stem . Because playing a single game of tennis is a one-time "Polaroid snapshot" event, we must use the Aorist Stem! In the last lesson, you learned how to find the Aorist Stem by changing the final consonant. - παίζω (I play) ➔ παίξ- 2. Assembling the Simple Future To build the Simple Future, we need three ingredients: 1. The future word: Θα 2. The Simple Stem: παίξ- 3. The Endings... wait, which endings do we use? We are talking about the Future, not the Past! So we CANNOT use the Past tense endings (-α, -ες, -ε...). Instead, we use the easiest endings in the world: the Present Tense endings you already know (-ω, -εις, -ει...)! Look at how beautifully it comes together: Pronoun Component 1 Component 2 Component 3 Final Result English (Εγώ) Θα παίξ- -ω θα παίξω I will play (Εσύ) Θα παίξ- -εις θα παίξεις You will play (Αυτό/ή/ό) Θα παίξ- -ει θα παίξει He will play (Εμείς) Θα παίξ- -ουμε θα παίξουμε We will play (Εσείς) Θα παίξ- -ετε θα παίξετε You will play (Αυτοί/ές/ά) Θα παίξ- -ουν(ε) θα παίξουν They will play [!CAUTION] No Augments allowed! Remember the letter ε we added to the front of past tense verbs (έπαιζα)? You MUST NOT add it to the Future tense! The augment έ- is exclusively a marker of the Past tense. The Simple Future only uses the pure stem ( παίξ- ). 3. Side-by-Side Comparison Let's look at the two Futures side-by-side using the verb γράφω (I write). Continuous Stem : γράφ- Simple Stem : γράψ- Scenario A: The Author Θα γράφω ένα βιβλίο κάθε χρόνο. (I will be writing a book every year.) Repeated habit ➔ Continuous Stem. Scenario B: The Student Θα γράψω τις ασκήσεις μου αύριο. (I will write my exercises tomorrow.) One-time completed action ➔ Simple Stem. It's that easy! Just swap the letter 'φ' for the letter 'ψ', and you have completely changed the aspect of the sentence. 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the Simple Future is used for single, completed events in the future. [ ] I know how to combine Θα + Simple Stem + Present Endings . [ ] I know to never use the past tense augment (έ-) when forming the Future.

simple-subjunctive.title: simple-subjunctive
simple-subjunctive.content: The Simple Subjunctive (Η Απλή Υποτακτική) In Level A2 (Unit 10), you learned how to translate sentences like "I want to go " by using the particle να + a conjugated verb: "Θέλω να πάω". As you might have guessed by now, the Subjunctive Mood has two Aspects. Continuous Subjunctive: Θέλω να παίζω. Simple Subjunctive: Θέλω να παίξω. Look at how similar the Subjunctive is to the Future tense you just learned! - What particle do you use for the Future? Θα . - What particle do you use for the Subjunctive? Να . - Does everything else stay exactly the same? Yes! 1. When do I use the Simple Subjunctive? This gives English speakers a lot of trouble because we don't have aspect in the infinitive. "I want to eat" is the only option in English. In Greek, you must ask yourself: "Do I want to perform this action over and over again as a habit, or do I want to do it right now and finish it?" 90% of the time, when we talk about wanting or needing to do something, we want to do it ONCE. Look at these two scenarios: Scenario A: The Dreamer (Continuous) Θέλω να παίζω πιάνο. (I want to be playing the piano. / I want to be a piano player .) Ongoing state/habit ➔ Continuous Stem. Scenario B: The Concert (Simple) Θέλω να σου παίξω ένα τραγούδι. (I want to play you a song.) One-time completed action ➔ Simple Stem. Because we usually ask people to do specific, single actions ("Can you read this?", "I need to sleep"), the Simple Subjunctive is overwhelmingly more common in daily speech. You will hear it constantly! 2. Let's Practice Assembling It The recipe for the Simple Subjunctive is exactly the same as the Simple Future. Να + Simple Stem + Present Endings . Let's use the verb διαβάζω (I read) ➔ Simple Stem: διαβάσ- . Pronoun The Formula Final Result English (Εγώ) θέλω να + διαβάσ- + -ω Θέλω να διαβάσω I want to read (Εσύ) πρέπει να + διαβάσ- + -εις Πρέπει να διαβάσεις You must read (Αυτός) μπορεί να + διαβάσ- + -ει Μπορεί να διαβάσει He can read (Εμείς) θέλουμε να + διαβάσ- + -ουμε Θέλουμε να διαβάσουμε We want to read (Εσείς) πρέπει να + διαβάσ- + -ετε Πρέπει να διαβάσετε You must read (Αυτοί) μπορούν να + διαβάσ- + -ουν Μπορούν να διαβάσουν They can read (Remember: the first verb, like Θέλω or Μπορώ, must also be conjugated to match the pronoun!) 3. The Ultimate Rule: NO AUGMENTS It is very tempting to look at the Aorist (Past Tense) word for "I wrote" ( έγραψα ) and try to use it with να . Do not do this! Never say: Θέλω να έγραψα . The Augment ( έ- ) is strictly a Past Tense marker. It means "this happened in the past". You cannot want to do something in the past! You must strip away the augment, take the pure Simple Stem ( γράψ- ), and use the Present Tense endings ( -ω, -εις, -ει... ). ➔ Θέλω να γράψω. 4. The Final Form: Negative Sentences How do you translate "I don't want to go"? You know that δεν makes verbs negative. But you just learned that να also sits right in front of the verb. Wait, two particles? δεν + να ? No! Greek has a special rule: You cannot use δεν directly next to να . Instead, the negative particle δεν transforms into a special word: μη(ν) . The Μην Rule Whenever you want to negate a Subjunctive verb, use μη(ν) instead of δεν . Θέλω να μην πάω . (I want to not go ). Πρέπει να μην καπνίζεις . (You must not smoke ). (Just like δεν and την, μην drops its final 'ν' if the next word starts with a soft consonant). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that the Simple Subjunctive is the most common way to translate English infinitives ("to play", "to run"). [ ] I know how to combine Να + Simple Stem + Present Endings . [ ] I know to never use the past tense augment (έ-) when forming the Subjunctive. [ ] I know that the negative word for the Subjunctive is μη(ν) , not δεν!

social-expressions.title: social-expressions
social-expressions.content: Social Expressions This lesson covers common expressions used in social settings, including answering the phone and social events. On the Phone Εμπρός; (Hello? - answering) Ναι; (Yes? / Hello?) Ποιος είναι; (Who is it?) Ένα λεπτό. (One minute.) Congratulations & Wishes Χρόνια Πολλά! (Happy Birthday/Anniversary!) Συγχαρητήρια! (Congratulations!) Καλή επιτυχία! (Good luck!) Περαστικά! (Get well soon!) Drinks & Food Στην υγειά μας! (Cheers!) Γειά μας! (Cheers!) Καλή όρεξη! (Bon Appétit!) Cultural Note Χρόνια Πολλά is the most common wish in Greece. It is used for birthdays, name days, weddings, Christmas, Easter, and almost every national holiday!

subjunctive-1.title: Unit 10: The Subjunctive Mood
subjunctive-1.content: The Subjunctive Mood (Η Υποτακτική) Welcome to a uniquely Greek problem. In English, we have something called the infinitive . It's the base form of a verb, usually starting with "to". - I want to go . - We need to sleep . - They can to run (wait, we say "they can run", but you get the idea). Here is the problem: Greek does not have an infinitive. There is no word for "to go". There is only "I go", "You go", "He goes". So how do you translate a sentence like "I want to go"? 1. The Magic Word: Να Because Greek verbs are always conjugated (they always have an ending pointing to a person), Greek solves the "to go" problem by using the little word να (na). Να acts like the English word "to". But instead of being followed by a base verb, it is followed by a fully conjugated verb! Let's look at the literal translation: - English: I want to go . - Greek (Literal): I want that I go . - Greek (Actual): Θέλω να πάω. Let's try another one: - English: Do you want to play ? - Greek (Literal): Do you want that you play ? - Greek (Actual): Θέλεις να παίζεις; Notice what happened there? Both verbs have to match the person doing the action! If I want, and I am the one going, then BOTH verbs must end in -ω : (Θέλ ω να πά ω ). If You want, and You are the one playing, BOTH verbs must end in -εις : (Θέλ εις να παίζ εις ;). This is called the Subjunctive Mood . 2. When to Use "Να" You will use να constantly. Anytime you have two verbs stacked next to each other in English, you will almost certainly need a να separating them in Greek. Here are the most common verbs that trigger the Subjunctive: - Θέλω να... (I want to...) - Μπορώ να... (I can...) - Πρέπει να... (I must...) - Ξέρω να... (I know how to...) Μπορούμε να κάνουμε κάτι; (Can we do something?) Notice the matching endings: Bορ ούμε and κάν ουμε . Ξέρει να μιλάει αγγλικά. (He knows how to speak English.) Notice the matching endings: Ξέρ ει and μιλά ει . 3. The Standalone "Na" (Commands & Wishes) The word να is so powerful that it doesn't even need a first verb! If you start a sentence with να , it turns the verb into a strong suggestion, a wish, or a mild command. Να προσέχεις! (Take care! / Careful!) Να είσαι καλά. (Be well. / The most common way to say thank you/goodbye! ) Να πάμε; (Shall we go?) The Ultimate Question: Τι να κάνω; If you are confused, lost, or asking for advice, this is the only phrase you need: Τι να κάνω; (What should I do? / literally: What that I do?). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I understand that Greek does not have an "infinitive" verb (like "to run"). [ ] I know that Greek uses να + a conjugated verb to translate things like "I want to run". [ ] I know that if the "want-er" and the "run-er" are the same person, both verbs must have the exact same ending (Θέλ ω να τρέχ ω ).

subjunctive-2.title: Unit 31: Advanced Subjunctive Usage
subjunctive-2.content: Advanced Subjunctive Usage In Level A2 and B1, you learned the golden rule of the Subjunctive: Whenever you want to use an "infinitive" (to go, to eat), you use the word να and a conjugated verb instead. - Θέλω να πάω (I want to go). But the Subjunctive Mood in Greek is far more powerful than just replacing the infinitive. It is the mood of emotion, subjectivity, doubt, and fear. In Level C1, you must learn the "hidden" triggers of the subjunctive. 1. Verbs of Emotion (Χαίρομαι που / να) When you express an emotion about a fact, you typically use που (that). - Χαίρομαι που ήρθες. (I am glad that you came). However, when you express an emotion about doing an action , you use the Subjunctive ( να ). - Χαίρομαι να σε βλέπω. (I am glad to see you). - Μ' αρέσει να πίνω καφέ. (I like to drink coffee). 2. Expressions of Fear (Φοβάμαι μη) This is one of the strangest rules for English speakers. When you are afraid that something bad will happen, Greek uses the negative subjunctive particle μη(ν) instead of να ! In English, you say: "I am afraid that it will rain". In Greek, you say: "I am afraid lest it rains" (Φοβάμαι μην βρέξει). Φοβάμαι μην πέσεις. (I am afraid you might fall). Φοβάται μην αργήσουμε. (He is afraid we might be late). Why? Because the underlying logic is: "I am afraid [and I wish that you do] not fall." (Note: If you are afraid of a confirmed, existing fact, you just use "που" or "ότι": Φοβάμαι ότι έχεις δίκιο - I am afraid that you are right). 3. Doubt and Uncertainty (Αμφιβάλλω αν) When expressing doubt, Greek doesn't use "να". It uses the conditional word Αν (If / Whether). Δεν ξέρω αν θα έρθει. (I don't know if/whether he will come). Αμφιβάλλω αν είναι αλήθεια. (I doubt if/whether it is true). 4. Final Clauses (Purpose: Για να) When you want to say "In order to...", you must use Για να + Subjunctive. Πάω στην Ελλάδα για να μάθω τη γλώσσα. (I am going to Greece in order to learn the language). Μιλάω αργά για να καταλάβεις. (I am speaking slowly so that you understand). Notice in the second example, the subjects are different (I am speaking, so that YOU understand). In English, this requires clunky grammar. In Greek, "Για να" handles it effortlessly just by conjugating the second verb! 5. Indefinite Pronouns + Subjunctive Finally, the Subjunctive is heavily triggered by "Whatever", "Whoever", and "Wherever" phrases when referring to an unknown future event. Ό,τι και να γίνει... (Whatever happens...) Όπου και να πας... (Wherever you go...) Όποιος και να είσαι... (Whoever you are...) 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know that verbs of emotion followed by an action use να ( Χαίρομαι να σε βλέπω ). [ ] I know the strange rule that "Fear" triggers the negative μην ( Φοβάμαι μην πέσεις ). [ ] I know that Doubt triggers αν ( Δεν ξέρω αν... ). [ ] I know that "In order to" translates to Για να . [ ] I can use "Ο,τι και να..." for "Whatever...".

subjunctive-advanced.content: Advanced Subjunctive Usage Content for Advanced Subjunctive Usage goes here.

tenses.title: tenses
tenses.content: Verb Tenses Greek verb tenses are divided based on when the action happens (Past, Present, Future) and how it happens (Continuous vs. Simple/Instant). Present (Ενεστώτας) παίζω I am playing / I play Future (Μέλλοντας) θα παίζω I will be playing (continuous)θα παίξω I will play (once) Past (Παρελθόν) έπαιζα I was playing (Imperfect)έπαιξα I played (Aorist) Perfect (Παρακείμενος) έχω παίξει I have played The Aspect Rule In Greek, the most important distinction is between Continuous (actions that happen repeatedly or over time) and Simple (actions that happen once). This distinction is reflected in most tenses!

verb-groups-b.title: Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2
verb-groups-b.content: Type B Verbs (The Accented Omegas) In Unit 4, you mastered Type A verbs (like διαβάζω and κάνω ). You learned that they are easy to spot because their final -ω does not have an accent mark on it. Now it is time to meet the second major team of verbs verbs: Type B . You know you are dealing with a Type B verb when the dictionary form ends in an Omega that has an accent mark on it ( -ώ ). 1. Why are they different? Type B verbs are rebels. When you conjugate them, they don't just add a regular ending (like -εις or -ει ). They sneak an extra vowel (usually an α or an ου ) right before the ending. Because of this, Type B verbs are subdivided into two families: B1 and B2 . B1 verbs love the letter α . B2 verbs love the sound ου . 2. The B1 Family (The "Alpha" Lovers) This is the biggest group of Type B verbs. These verbs often have an alternate dictionary spelling ending in -άω . If you see a verb ending in -άω , it is 100% guaranteed to be a B1 verb! Common B1 Verbs: - αγαπώ / αγαπάω (I love) - μιλώ / μιλάω (I speak) - ρωτώ / ρωτάω (I ask) - ξυπνώ / ξυπνάω (I wake up) Let's look at the conjugation for αγαπάω (I love). Notice how the letter α dominates almost every ending! Pronoun B1 Ending Example (αγαπάω) (Εγώ) -άω (or -ώ) αγαπ άω (Εσύ) -άς αγαπ άς (Αυτό/ή/ό) -άει (or -ά) αγαπ άει (Εμείς) -άμε αγαπ άμε (Εσείς) -άτε αγαπ άτε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -άνε (or -ούν) αγαπ άνε [!TIP] Formal vs. Informal You'll notice some alternate endings in parentheses above (like αγαπώ instead of αγαπάω ). The -άω / -άει / -άνε endings are the modern, everyday, spoken formats. The -ώ / -ά / -ούν endings are considered more formal and literary (Katharevousa remnants). We highly recommend you stick to the -άω format! It is much easier to pronounce and remember: just think "Alpha"! 3. The B2 Family (The "Oo" Lovers) B2 verbs are much less common than B1s, but they include some incredibly important words. Unlike B1s, B2 verbs never have an alternate "-άω" spelling. They only end in -ώ . Common B2 Verbs: - μπορώ (I can) - ζω (I live) - τηλεφωνώ (I call) - οδηγώ (I drive) Let's look at the conjugation for μπορώ (I can). Notice how the sound ου ( [ee] ) dominates the plural endings! Pronoun B2 Ending Example (μπορώ) (Εγώ) -ώ βορ ώ (Εσύ) -είς βορ είς (Αυτό/ή/ό) -εί βορ εί (Εμείς) -ούμε βορ ούμε (Εσείς) -είτε βορ είτε (Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ούν(ε) βορ ούν(ε) Notice something? The B2 endings look exactly like the Type A endings, but with an ε or an ου aggressively jammed in front of them! 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I can tell a Type A verb ( κάνω ) from a Type B verb ( μιλώ ). [ ] I know that B1 verbs love the letter α ( μιλάω, μιλάς, μιλάει ). [ ] I know that B2 verbs love the sound ου in the plural ( μπορούμε, μπορούν ).

verb-groups.title: verb-groups
verb-groups.content: Groups of Verbs (Οι Συζυγίες) Now you know that a Greek verb is made of a Stem + an Ending. But not all verbs have the exact same endings. Greek verbs are split into two major teams, or Conjugations (Συζυγίες). How do you know which team a verb is on? You look at where the accent mark is in the dictionary form (the "I" form). 1. Type A Verbs (The Unaccented Omegas) These are the most common verbs in the Greek language. If the verb ends in an Omega ( -ω ), but the Omega does not have an accent mark on it, it is a Type A verb. The accent mark is sitting over the stem instead. π αί ζω (I play) τρ έ χω (I run) κ ά νω (I do / I make) διαβ ά ζω (I read / I study) Because these are the most fundamental verbs, we are going to spend this entire unit mastering the Type A endings. 2. Type B Verbs (The Accented Omegas) If the verb ends in an Omega, and the Omega does have an accent mark on it, it is a Type B verb. αγαπ ώ (I love) μιλ ώ (I speak) ξυπν ώ (I wake up) τηλεφων ώ (I call) Type B verbs have a slightly different set of endings (they often sneak an extra 'α' or 'ου' into the ending). We won't worry about them until Unit 9 (so you can relax for now!). [!TIP] Warning: The Alternates Many Type B verbs have an alternate spelling where they end in -άω instead of -ώ . For example: αγαπώ and αγαπάω mean exactly the same thing. Whether you see αγαπώ or αγαπάω , it's still a Type B verb. See the accent mark? It's on the ending! 3. Type C Verbs (The Passive "Omai's") There is a third, very weird group of verbs. These verbs don't end in -ω at all! They end in -ομαι (o-me) . έρχ ομαι (I come) γίν ομαι (I become) κοιμ άμαι (I sleep) These verbs are incredibly important, but they require a completely different set of endings and grammar rules. This is called the Passive Voice (or "Deponent Verbs"). We will dedicate a massive part of B1 (Unit 18) to conquering these. For the rest of level A1, we are only going to focus on Type A verbs! 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist Look at the verb: διαβάζω (I read). - Does it end in -ω? Yes. - Is there an accent mark on the -ω? No. - Then it is a Type A verb! Look at the verb: μπορώ (I can). - Does it end in -ω? Yes. - Is there an accent mark on the -ω? Yes! - Then it is a Type B verb! Look at the verb: εργάζομαι (I work). - Does it end in -ω? No. - It ends in -ομαι. It belongs to the Passive group. If you can sort those three verbs into their correct buckets, you are ready for the next lesson!

verb-to-be.title: Unit 3: Being & Having
verb-to-be.content: The Verb 'To Be' (Είμαι) To build a real sentence, we need our first verb. And just like in English, the most important verb in the Greek language is the verb To Be (I am, you are, he is...). In Greek, the dictionary form of this verb is είμαι (í-me) . 1. The Personal Pronouns Before we conjugate the verb, we need to know the words for I, You, He, She, etc. These are called Personal Pronouns . English Greek Pronunciation I Εγώ (e-gó) You (singular/informal) Εσύ (e-sí) He She It Αυτός Αυτή Αυτό (af-tós) (af-tí) (af-tó) We Εμείς (e-mís) You (plural/formal) Εσείς (e-sís) They (masculine) They (feminine) They (neuter) Αυτοί Αυτές Αυτά (af-tí) (af-tés) (af-tá) [!NOTE] Did you notice something? The words for He, She, It, They are completely identical to the gender rules you just learned in Unit 2! - Αυτός ends in -ος (Masculine) - Αυτή ends in -η (Feminine) - Αυτό ends in -ο (Neuter). Also notice that Εμείς (We) and Εσείς (You plural) rhyme perfectly! 2. Conjugating "Είμαι" (I am) Now let's pair these pronouns with the verb. Pronoun Verb English Pronunciation Εγώ είμαι I am (í-me) Εσύ είσαι You are (í-se) Αυτός/ή/ό είναι He/She/It is (í-ne) Εμείς είμαστε We are (í-mas-te) Εσείς είστε You are (ís-te) Αυτοί/ές/ά είναι They are (í-ne) [!TIP] The Best News Ever: "He is" = "They are"! Look at the table again. The word for "He is" ( είναι ) is exactly the same word as "They are" ( είναι ). In Greek, the 3rd person singular and the 3rd person plural of the verb "To Be" are identical. 3. The Pro-Drop Rule (We don't need pronouns!) Here is one of the most important concepts in Greek: You do not need to say the pronoun. In English, if you walk into a room and say "Am hungry," people will look at you funny. You must say " I am hungry." In Greek, the ending of the verb already tells everyone who is speaking. The word είμαι only means "I am". It cannot mean "You are" or "He is". Therefore, Greeks rarely use the words Εγώ , Εσύ , Εμείς , etc., unless they want to emphasize something. English : I am George. Greek (Robotic) : Εγώ είμαι ο Γιώργος. Greek (Natural) : Είμαι ο Γιώργος. English : Are you Maria? Greek (Robotic) : Εσύ είσαι η Μαρία; Greek (Natural) : Είσαι η Μαρία; 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I know how to say I, You, He/She/It, We, They. [ ] I know the conjugation of είμαι (είμαι, είσαι, είναι, είμαστε, είστε, είναι). [ ] I know that Greeks usually drop the pronoun because the verb ending tells you who is doing the action.

verbs.title: Unit 4: Intro to Verbs (Type A)
verbs.content: Introduction to Verbs (Τα Ρήματα) You have arrived at Unit 4. You can read, you understand how nouns change their shape, and you know the pronouns. It is time to start making things happen. It is time for Verbs . A verb is an "action word" (run, play, sleep, want). In English, verbs are very simple. I play, you play, we play, they play. In Greek, verbs are the absolute workhorses of the sentence. They change their shape constantly to tell you who is doing the action, when they are doing it, and even how they are doing it. The Anatomy of a Greek Verb Every Greek verb is made up of two parts: the Stem and the Ending . Think of it like a tree. The Stem is the trunk—it never changes, and it holds the core meaning of the word. The Ending is the leaves—they change depending on the season (or in this case, depending on who is doing the action). Let's look at the dictionary form of the verb "to play": παίζω (pé-zo) The Stem is παίζ- The Ending is -ω The Secret of the Omega (-ω) Almost every single active verb in the Greek dictionary ends in the letter Omega ( -ω ). This is the "I" ending. If you see a verb ending in -ω , you instantly know it means "I [do something]". - παίζω (I play) - τρέχω (I run) - γράφω (I write) How Conjugation Works When we want to change the "doer" from I to You or We , we simply chop off the -ω ending and glue on a new one. This is called conjugation. This is exactly why Greeks don't use pronouns (I, You, He) most of the time! If you want to say "We play", you take the stem ( παίζ- ) and add the "We" ending ( -ουμε ). ➔ παίζουμε (We play). If you want to say "You play", you take the stem ( παίζ- ) and add the "You" ending ( -εις ). ➔ παίζεις (You play). See how simple it is? The stem stays the same, and the ending does all the heavy lifting! 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist [ ] I understand that a Greek verb is made of a rigid Stem and a flexible Ending . [ ] I know that the dictionary form of almost every verb ends in -ω . [ ] I know that -ω specifically means "I" am doing the action. [ ] I understand that we conjugate verbs by chopping off the -ω and adding new endings.

voices-moods.title: voices-moods
voices-moods.content: Voices and Moods Greek verbs have two Voices (who performs/receives the action) and several Moods (the attitude of the speaker towards the action). Voices (Φωνές) Ενεργητική (Active): The subject performs the action. Εγώ πλένω (I wash). Παθητική (Passive): The subject receives the action or acts on itself. Εγώ πλένομαι (I am being washed / I wash myself). Moods (Εγκλίσεις) Οριστική (Indicative): Facts and reality. Το παιδί παίζει (The child plays). Υποτακτική (Subjunctive): Wishes, possibilities, and "should". Often uses να . Θέλω να παίξω (I want to play). Προστακτική (Imperative): Commands and requests. Διάβασε! (Read!). Special Forms Greek also has Participles (Μετοχή) ending in -οντας (like "-ing" in English) and an Infinitive form (used in perfect tenses).

vowels-consonants.title: vowels-consonants
vowels-consonants.content: Vowels and Consonants Now that you can read syllables, it is crucial to understand the difference between Vowels and Consonants . Why? Because in Greek, the accent mark (τονισμός) can only be placed on a vowel. If you don't know your vowels, you won't know where to put the accent mark! 1. The 7 Vowels (Φωνήεντα) Greek has exactly seven vowels. However, because of the way the language evolved over thousands of years, these seven vowels only make five distinct sounds . Α α Sounds like [a] Ε ε Sounds like [e] Η η Sounds like [ee] Ι ι Sounds like [ee] Υ υ Sounds like [ee] Ο ο Sounds like [o] Ω ω Sounds like [o] The Spelling "Cheat Sheet" You might be wondering: "If Η, Ι, and Υ all sound exactly the same, how do I know which one to use when spelling?" There are strict grammatical rules that govern this, which you will naturally absorb. But here is a quick "cheat sheet" that works 80% of the time: Ι (Iota) is the "default" [ee] . Most root words inside the dictionary use it (e.g., φ ί λος = friend). Η (Eta) is mostly used at the absolute end of feminine nouns (e.g., η αγάπ η = the love). Υ (Upsilon) is the rarest and is mostly found in words derived from Ancient Greek (e.g., υ πολογιστής = computer). Ο (Omicron) is the default [o] . Ω (Omega) is mostly used at the absolute end of verbs (e.g., θέλ ω = I want). 2. The 17 Consonants (Σύμφωνα) Everything else in the alphabet is a consonant. Β, Γ, Δ, Ζ, Θ, Κ, Λ, Μ, Ν, Ξ, Π, Ρ, Σ, Τ, Φ, Χ, Ψ Unlike English, where "Y" can sometimes act as a vowel, Greek consonants are always consonants. They never carry the accent mark. Voiced vs. Voiceless Consonants You don't need to memorize this right now, but it's helpful to know that Greek consonants are split into two teams: Voiced (your vocal cords vibrate) and Voiceless (just air). Voiced : β, γ, δ, ζ, λ, μ, ν, ρ Voiceless : θ, κ, ξ, π, σ, τ, φ, χ, ψ [!CAUTION] Why does this matter? Remember the special double letters αυ and ευ from Lesson 2? - If they are followed by a Voiceless consonant, they sound like an "F" (αυτοκίνητο ➔ a f tokinito). - If they are followed by a Voiced consonant, they sound like a "V" (αύριο ➔ a v rio). 🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist Before moving to the next lesson, make sure you understand: - [ ] There are 7 vowels but only 5 sounds. - [ ] Η, Ι, Υ all sound like "ee". - [ ] Ο, Ω both sound like "o". - [ ] The accent mark always goes on one of the vowels.
